addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- compound-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Visually compares live UI implementation against Figma designs and provides detailed feedback on discrepancies. Use after writing or modifying HTML/CSS/React components to verify design fidelity. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:design-iterator: Iteratively refines UI design through N screenshot-analyze-improve cycles. Use PROACTIVELY when design changes aren't coming together after 1-2 attempts, or when user requests iterative refinement. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:figma-design-sync: Detects and fixes visual differences between a web implementation and its Figma design. Use iteratively when syncing implementation to match Figma specs. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:docs:ankane-readme-writer: Creates or updates README files following Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. Use when writing gem documentation with imperative voice, concise prose, and standard section ordering. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:best-practices-researcher: Researches and synthesizes external best practices, documentation, and examples for any technology or framework. Use when you need industry standards, community conventions, or implementation guidance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:framework-docs-researcher: Gathers comprehensive documentation and best practices for frameworks, libraries, or dependencies. Use when you need official docs, version-specific constraints, or implementation patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:git-history-analyzer: Performs archaeological analysis of git history to trace code evolution, identify contributors, and understand why code patterns exist. Use when you need historical context for code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:issue-intelligence-analyst: Fetches and analyzes GitHub issues to surface recurring themes, pain patterns, and severity trends. Use when understanding a project's issue landscape, analyzing bug patterns for ideation, or summarizing what users are reporting. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:learnings-researcher: Searches docs/solutions/ for relevant past solutions by frontmatter metadata. Use before implementing features or fixing problems to surface institutional knowledge and prevent repeated mistakes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:repo-research-analyst: Conducts thorough research on repository structure, documentation, conventions, and implementation patterns. Use when onboarding to a new codebase or understanding project conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:agent-native-reviewer: Reviews code to ensure agent-native parity — any action a user can take, an agent can also take. Use after adding UI features, agent tools, or system prompts. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:architecture-strategist: Analyzes code changes from an architectural perspective for pattern compliance and design integrity. Use when reviewing PRs, adding services, or evaluating structural refactors. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:code-simplicity-reviewer: Final review pass to ensure code is as simple and minimal as possible. Use after implementation is complete to identify YAGNI violations and simplification opportunities. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-integrity-guardian: Reviews database migrations, data models, and persistent data code for safety. Use when checking migration safety, data constraints, transaction boundaries, or privacy compliance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-migration-expert: Validates data migrations, backfills, and production data transformations against reality. Use when PRs involve ID mappings, column renames, enum conversions, or schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:deployment-verification-agent: Produces Go/No-Go deployment checklists with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Use when PRs touch production data, migrations, or risky data changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:dhh-rails-reviewer: Brutally honest Rails code review from DHH's perspective. Use when reviewing Rails code for anti-patterns, JS framework contamination, or violations of Rails conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:julik-frontend-races-reviewer: Reviews JavaScript and Stimulus code for race conditions, timing issues, and DOM lifecycle problems. Use after implementing or modifying frontend controllers or async UI code. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-python-reviewer: Reviews Python code with an extremely high quality bar for Pythonic patterns, type safety, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Python modules. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-rails-reviewer: Reviews Rails code with an extremely high quality bar for conventions, clarity, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Rails components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-typescript-reviewer: Reviews TypeScript code with an extremely high quality bar for type safety, modern patterns, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new TypeScript components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:pattern-recognition-specialist: Analyzes code for design patterns, anti-patterns, naming conventions, and duplication. Use when checking codebase consistency or verifying new code follows established patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:performance-oracle: Analyzes code for performance bottlenecks, algorithmic complexity, database queries, memory usage, and scalability. Use after implementing features or when performance concerns arise. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:schema-drift-detector: Detects unrelated schema.rb changes in PRs by cross-referencing against included migrations. Use when reviewing PRs with database schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:security-sentinel: Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:bug-reproduction-validator: Systematically reproduces and validates bug reports to confirm whether reported behavior is an actual bug. Use when you receive a bug report or issue that needs verification. (Tools: All tools)
- compound-engineering:workflow:lint: Use this agent when you need to run linting and code quality checks on Ruby and ERB files. Run before pushing to origin. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:pr-comment-resolver: Addresses PR review comments by implementing requested changes and reporting resolutions. Use when code review feedback needs to be resolved with code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:spec-flow-analyzer: Analyzes specifications and feature descriptions for user flow completeness and gap identification. Use when a spec, plan, or feature description needs flow analysis, edge case discovery, or requirements validation. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- context7-mcp: This skill should be used when the user asks about libraries, frameworks, API references, or needs code examples. Activates for setup questions, code generation involving libraries, or mentions of specific frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, Prisma, Supabase, etc.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- code-changes: Orchestration workflow for any task that ends in code changes: issue analysis, pull request review, feature implementation, bug fixes, refactors, or fleshing out an idea. MUST be invoked at the start of such a task, before reading or writing any code. Defines how to analyze first, gate on user approval, plan, pick the right executor model, delegate and supervise subagents, verify, and deliver.
- conventional-commit: Workflow for generating conventional commit messages following the Conventional Commits specification. MUST be invoked every time a commit is created. Guides construction of standardized commit messages with correct type, scope, description, body, and footer.
- golang: Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing, reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
- markdown: Markdown formatting rules for this project. Apply when writing or editing any .md or .mdx file, including documentation and website content.
- powershell: PowerShell cmdlet conventions for this project. Apply when writing or reviewing any .ps1 or module file.
- writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- compound-engineering:agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- compound-engineering:agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:andrew-kane-gem-writer: This skill should be used when writing Ruby gems following Andrew Kane's proven patterns and philosophy. It applies when creating new Ruby gems, refactoring existing gems, designing gem APIs, or when clean, minimal, production-ready Ruby library code is needed. Triggers on requests like "create a gem", "write a Ruby library", "design a gem API", or mentions of Andrew Kane's style.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue before writing a right-sized requirements document and planning implementation. Use for feature ideas, problem framing, when the user says 'let's brainstorm', or when they want to think through options before deciding what to build. Also use when a user describes a vague or ambitious feature request, asks 'what should we build', 'help me think through X', presents a problem with multiple valid solutions, or seems unsure about scope or direction — even if they don't explicitly ask to brainstorm.
- compound-engineering:ce-compound: Document a recently solved problem to compound your team's knowledge
- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded improvement ideas for the current project. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising improvements, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong project directions before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on this project', 'surprise me with improvements', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated project improvement suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.
- compound-engineering:ce-plan: Transform feature descriptions into well-structured project plans following conventions
- compound-engineering:ce-review: Perform exhaustive code reviews using multi-agent analysis, ultra-thinking, and worktrees
- compound-engineering:ce-work: Execute work plans efficiently while maintaining quality and finishing features
- compound-engineering:claude-permissions-optimizer: Optimize Claude Code permissions by finding safe Bash commands from session history and auto-applying them to settings.json. Can run from any coding agent but targets Claude Code specifically. Use when experiencing permission fatigue, too many permission prompts, wanting to optimize permissions, or needing to set up allowlists. Triggers on "optimize permissions", "reduce permission prompts", "allowlist commands", "too many permission prompts", "permission fatigue", "permission setup", or complaints about clicking approve too often.
- compound-engineering:deepen-plan: Enhance a plan with parallel research agents for each section to add depth, best practices, and implementation details
- compound-engineering:dhh-rails-style: This skill should be used when writing Ruby and Rails code in DHH's distinctive 37signals style. It applies when writing Ruby code, Rails applications, creating models, controllers, or any Ruby file. Triggers on Ruby/Rails code generation, refactoring requests, code review, or when the user mentions DHH, 37signals, Basecamp, HEY, or Campfire style. Embodies REST purity, fat models, thin controllers, Current attributes, Hotwire patterns, and the "clarity over cleverness" philosophy.
- compound-engineering:document-review: This skill should be used to refine requirements or plan documents before proceeding to the next workflow step. It applies when a requirements document or plan document exists and the user wants to improve it.
- compound-engineering:dspy-ruby: Build type-safe LLM applications with DSPy.rb — Ruby's programmatic prompt framework with signatures, modules, agents, and optimization. Use when implementing predictable AI features, creating LLM signatures and modules, configuring language model providers, building agent systems with tools, optimizing prompts, or testing LLM-powered functionality in Ruby applications.
- compound-engineering:every-style-editor: This skill should be used when reviewing or editing copy to ensure adherence to Every's style guide. It provides a systematic line-by-line review process for grammar, punctuation, mechanics, and style guide compliance.
- compound-engineering:feature-video: Record a video walkthrough of a feature and add it to the PR description. Use when a PR needs a visual demo for reviewers, when the user asks to demo a feature, create a PR video, record a walkthrough, show what changed visually, or add a video to a pull request.
- compound-engineering:gemini-imagegen: This skill should be used when generating and editing images using the Gemini API (Nano Banana Pro). It applies when creating images from text prompts, editing existing images, applying style transfers, generating logos with text, creating stickers, product mockups, or any image generation/manipulation task. Supports text-to-image, image editing, multi-turn refinement, and composition from multiple reference images.
- compound-engineering:git-worktree: This skill manages Git worktrees for isolated parallel development. It handles creating, listing, switching, and cleaning up worktrees with a simple interactive interface, following KISS principles.
- compound-engineering:proof: Create, edit, comment on, and share markdown documents via Proof's web API and local bridge. Use when asked to "proof", "share a doc", "create a proof doc", "comment on a document", "suggest edits", "review in proof", or when given a proofeditor.ai URL.
- compound-engineering:rclone: Upload, sync, and manage files across cloud storage providers using rclone. Use when uploading files (images, videos, documents) to S3, Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2, Google Drive, Dropbox, or any S3-compatible storage. Triggers on "upload to S3", "sync to cloud", "rclone", "backup files", "upload video/image to bucket", or requests to transfer files to remote storage.
- compound-engineering:reproduce-bug: Systematically reproduce and investigate a bug from a GitHub issue. Use when the user provides a GitHub issue number or URL for a bug they want reproduced or investigated.
- compound-engineering:resolve-todo-parallel: Resolve all pending CLI todos using parallel processing, compound on lessons learned, then clean up completed todos.
- compound-engineering:test-browser: Run browser tests on pages affected by current PR or branch
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:frontend-slides: Create stunning, animation-rich HTML presentations from scratch or by converting PowerPoint files. Use when the user wants to build a presentation, convert a PPT/PPTX to web, or create slides for a talk/pitch. Helps non-designers discover their aesthetic through visual exploration rather than abstract choices.
- anthropic-skills:itdepends-offering: Use this skill whenever Jan (IT depends) needs to create a professional offering, proposal, quote, or commercial document as a Word (.docx) file. This skill extends the base docx skill with IT depends brand styling: the company logo, clean sans-serif typography, minimal layout with light gray label rows, and structured sections (scope, pricing, timeline, terms). Trigger on phrases like "create an offering", "make a proposal", "write a quote document", "draft a commercial proposal", or "create a Word offering for [client]". Always use this skill (not the generic docx skill) when the output is an IT depends branded offering or proposal.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:proposal-iteration-agent: Use this skill to start an interactive proposal development session. The agent asks intelligent, context-aware questions to gather all necessary information for crafting a professional proposal: client details, scope of work, pricing strategy, timeline, deliverables, and terms. The agent iterates with you, refines content, and maintains a structured proposal framework that can be exported to the itdepends-offering skill once finalized. Trigger on: "start a proposal",  "help me build a proposal", "iterate on proposal content", "gather proposal requirements", or "let's develop a proposal together".
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:spilberg-docs-style: Use this style guide whenever creating a Word document (.docx) for Spilberg — reports, technical documentation, assessments, proposals, or any client-facing deliverable that should look like it came from Spilberg (the IT Development & Testing staffing & consulting brand, part of Vibe Group). Trigger on phrases like "Spilberg report", "Spilberg document", "make this look like Spilberg", "branded for Spilberg", or whenever the user is producing a deliverable to share with Spilberg's clients or candidates. This skill defines Spilberg's brand colors, typography, logo usage, and full-page layout (including the signature green footer wordmark) so every document is instantly identifiable as Spilberg's own. Always combine with the base docx skill for the underlying docx-js mechanics.
- anthropic-skills:vibe-group-docs-style: Use this style guide when the user asks to generate a document for Vibe Group.
- anthropic-skills:writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The user wants to explore different approaches for building a comprehensive end-to-end test suite that validates oh-my-posh across all supported shells—bash, zsh, fish, pwsh, cmd/clink, nu, elvish, xonsh, and others—using a standard configuration with configurable features. They're looking for design options and architectural approaches rather than asking me to implement changes yet.

input.args: Design options for an end-to-end shell test suite (all supported shells) validating prompt behavior against a standard toggleable config

content.content: Launching skill: code-changes

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability:

| Role         | Capability tier                               | Owns                                      |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Orchestrator | Strongest reasoning model available           | Analysis, plan, supervision, verification |
| Implementer  | Mid-tier coder; small model for trivial edits | Executing one pinned, self-contained task |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs on an implementer-tier model, skip delegation: plan and
execute directly, still following every phase. Never delegate to a model more capable than the
current one.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   cheapest capable executor.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)


ARGUMENTS: Design options for an end-to-end shell test suite (all supported shells) validating prompt behavior against a standard toggleable config

1	# Phase 1 — Analyze
2	
3	Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
4	No code gets written or edited during this phase.
5	
6	## Gather the full context
7	
8	- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
9	  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
10	- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
11	  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
12	- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
13	  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).
14	
15	## Reproduce before theorizing
16	
17	Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
18	fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
19	hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.
20	
21	## Find the root cause in the code
22	
23	- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
24	  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
25	- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
26	  why it crashes.
27	- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.
28	
29	## Output of this phase
30	
31	A short analysis report to the user containing:
32	
33	1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
34	2. The proposed change and its scope.
35	3. What is intentionally out of scope.
36	4. Open questions, if any remain.
37	
38	## Stop gate
39	
40	Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
41	already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
42	Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.
43

file.content:
# Phase 1 — Analyze

Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
No code gets written or edited during this phase.

## Gather the full context

- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).

## Reproduce before theorizing

Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.

## Find the root cause in the code

- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
  why it crashes.
- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.

## Output of this phase

A short analysis report to the user containing:

1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
2. The proposed change and its scope.
3. What is intentionally out of scope.
4. Open questions, if any remain.

## Stop gate

Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.

file.content:
on:
  pull_request:
    paths-ignore:
      - 'README.md'
      - 'CONTRIBUTING.md'
      - 'COPYING'
      - 'website/**'
      - '.github/*.md'
      - '.github/FUNDING.yml'

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

name: Validate Code
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: ${{ github.workspace }}/src
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0
    - name: Install Go 🗳
      uses: ./.github/workflows/composite/bootstrap-go
    - name: Golang CI
      uses: golangci/golangci-lint-action@ba0d7d2ec06a0ea1cb5fa41b2e4a3ab91d21278a
      with:
        working-directory: src
    - name: Fieldalignment
      run: |
        go install golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/fieldalignment/cmd/fieldalignment@latest
        fieldalignment "./..."
    - name: Modernize
      run: |
        go install golang.org/x/tools/gopls/internal/analysis/modernize/cmd/modernize@latest
        modernize "./..."
    - name: Unit Tests
      run: go test "./..."

1	package shell
2	
3	import "fmt"
4	
5	type Features uint
6	
7	const (
8		Jobs Features = 1 << iota
9		Azure
10		PoshGit
11		LineError
12		Tooltips
13		Transient
14		FTCSMarks
15		Upgrade
16		Notice
17		PromptMark
18		RPrompt
19		CursorPositioning
20		Async
21		Streaming
22		KeyHandlers
23		VIMode
24		TransientRPrompt
25	)
26	
27	// getAllFeatures returns all defined feature flags by iterating through bit positions
28	func getAllFeatures() []Features {
29		var features []Features
30	
31		// Iterate through all possible bit positions
32		for i := range uint(32) { // 32 should be more than enough for our features
33			feature := Features(1 << i)
34	
35			// Stop when we reach a power of 2 greater than our highest defined feature
36			if feature > TransientRPrompt {
37				break
38			}
39	
40			// Add the feature if it's a power of 2 (valid feature flag)
41			if feature != 0 && (feature&(feature-1)) == 0 {
42				features = append(features, feature)
43			}
44		}
45	
46		return features
47	}
48	
49	func (f Features) Lines(shell string) Lines {
50		var lines Lines
51	
52		// Get all features dynamically
53		allFeatures := getAllFeatures()
54	
55		for _, feature := range allFeatures {
56			// Check if this feature is enabled in the Features bitmask
57			if uint(f)&uint(feature) == 0 {
58				continue
59			}
60	
61			var code Code
62	
63			switch shell {
64			case PWSH:
65				code = feature.Pwsh()
66			case ZSH:
67				code = feature.Zsh()
68			case BASH:
69				code = feature.Bash()
70			case ELVISH:
71				code = feature.Elvish()
72			case FISH:
73				code = feature.Fish()
74			case CMD:
75				code = feature.Cmd()
76			case NU:
77				code = feature.Nu()
78			case XONSH:
79				code = feature.Xonsh()
80			case YASH:
81				code = feature.Yash()
82			}
83	
84			if len(code) > 0 {
85				lines = append(lines, code)
86			}
87		}
88	
89		return lines
90	}
91	
92	func (f Features) String() string {
93		return fmt.Sprintf("%b", uint(f))
94	}
95

1	# Testing shells end-to-end
2	
3	Patterns for functionally driving omp's shell integrations, mostly in WSL (verified on aarch64,
4	zsh 5.9, fish 4.1.2).
5	
6	## WSL basics
7	
8	- WSL `/tmp` is wiped between separate `wsl.exe` invocations (instance auto-shutdown). Either make
9	  a test fully self-contained in ONE `wsl -e` call, or stage everything under `$HOME` (e.g.
10	  `~/omp-test`).
11	- From Git Bash, prefix `MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1` so `/mnt/c/...` arguments reach wsl.exe unmangled.
12	- Build for WSL either inside WSL (`/usr/local/go/bin/go`) or cross-compile from Windows
13	  (`GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64`). Shell scripts are embedded at build time - rebuild after every
14	  script edit, then regenerate the sourced init with `oh-my-posh init <shell> --print`.
15	- Differential testing: build the control binary from `git show HEAD:<file>` or via
16	  `git stash push -- <file>`; the fixed binary from the working tree.
17	- `pkill -f <pattern>` matches its own caller's command line when inlined - pick patterns that
18	  cannot appear in the runner script.
19	
20	## Getting a pty
21	
22	- `zsh -i` under plain `wsl -e` hangs before the first prompt (no foreground pty). Options:
23	  - `wsl -e script -qec 'zsh -i' /dev/null` - real pty, MONITOR enabled, job-control behavior
24	    testable. Run backgrounded with output to a file and poll a done-marker; foreground runs can
25	    wedge under the wsl relay.
26	  - `zmodload zsh/zpty` in a driver zsh - best for keystroke-level scenarios: `zpty omp zsh -i`,
27	    write raw keys with `zpty -w -n omp $'\x03'` (Ctrl+C delivers a real SIGINT through the pty),
28	    assert via state files written by in-session commands, drain output with `zpty -r` after exit.
29	    Note `script(1)` does NOT work inside zpty (typescript never appears).
30	- Per-shell `script(1)` quirks: bash prompt bytes are not relayed to stdout (probe `${PS1@P}`
31	  in-session); fish discards piped typeahead (in-session probes never run); zsh relays fine.
32	- Do not test the zsh serve path from a non-interactive `zsh script.zsh` - it hangs in
33	  `read -u fd -d $'\0' -t N` (the `-t` is ignored with `-d`, see [zsh](zsh.md)).
34	
35	## Driving vi-mode / keystroke scenarios (zpty pattern, verified 2026-07-14)
36	
37	- Harness pattern (built for #5992): stage a binary, config, zdot dirs, and zpty driver scripts
38	  under `$HOME` in WSL; one driver runs mode-aware ESC/Enter/Ctrl+C scenarios into a state log,
39	  another asserts the transient prompt renders. Always compare a fixed run against a control zdot
40	  (same setup, feature under test disabled).
41	- Keep probes mode-aware: with zsh-vi-mode, a line after a normal-mode accept starts in normal
42	  mode - prefix typed commands with `i` where needed, and remember a stray self-inserted `i`
43	  turns the probe into a failing command (useful as a broken-state detector: `last_status=127`).
44	
45	## Keeping a render alive
46	
47	- There is no shell-command segment type. To hold `omp stream`/a render open for a controllable
48	  window, point an `http` segment at a silent local TCP listener
49	  (`python3 -c 'socket...accept...sleep'`) with a large `http_timeout`; kill the listener to
50	  trigger the async update record.
51	
52	## Reading protocol streams
53	
54	- One `bufio.Scanner` per pipe, ever - a second scanner on the same pipe loses buffered data
55	  (use a shared reader helper in Go tests).
56

file.content:
# Testing shells end-to-end

Patterns for functionally driving omp's shell integrations, mostly in WSL (verified on aarch64,
zsh 5.9, fish 4.1.2).

## WSL basics

- WSL `/tmp` is wiped between separate `wsl.exe` invocations (instance auto-shutdown). Either make
  a test fully self-contained in ONE `wsl -e` call, or stage everything under `$HOME` (e.g.
  `~/omp-test`).
- From Git Bash, prefix `MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1` so `/mnt/c/...` arguments reach wsl.exe unmangled.
- Build for WSL either inside WSL (`/usr/local/go/bin/go`) or cross-compile from Windows
  (`GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64`). Shell scripts are embedded at build time - rebuild after every
  script edit, then regenerate the sourced init with `oh-my-posh init <shell> --print`.
- Differential testing: build the control binary from `git show HEAD:<file>` or via
  `git stash push -- <file>`; the fixed binary from the working tree.
- `pkill -f <pattern>` matches its own caller's command line when inlined - pick patterns that
  cannot appear in the runner script.

## Getting a pty

- `zsh -i` under plain `wsl -e` hangs before the first prompt (no foreground pty). Options:
  - `wsl -e script -qec 'zsh -i' /dev/null` - real pty, MONITOR enabled, job-control behavior
    testable. Run backgrounded with output to a file and poll a done-marker; foreground runs can
    wedge under the wsl relay.
  - `zmodload zsh/zpty` in a driver zsh - best for keystroke-level scenarios: `zpty omp zsh -i`,
    write raw keys with `zpty -w -n omp $'\x03'` (Ctrl+C delivers a real SIGINT through the pty),
    assert via state files written by in-session commands, drain output with `zpty -r` after exit.
    Note `script(1)` does NOT work inside zpty (typescript never appears).
- Per-shell `script(1)` quirks: bash prompt bytes are not relayed to stdout (probe `${PS1@P}`
  in-session); fish discards piped typeahead (in-session probes never run); zsh relays fine.
- Do not test the zsh serve path from a non-interactive `zsh script.zsh` - it hangs in
  `read -u fd -d $'\0' -t N` (the `-t` is ignored with `-d`, see [zsh](zsh.md)).

## Driving vi-mode / keystroke scenarios (zpty pattern, verified 2026-07-14)

- Harness pattern (built for #5992): stage a binary, config, zdot dirs, and zpty driver scripts
  under `$HOME` in WSL; one driver runs mode-aware ESC/Enter/Ctrl+C scenarios into a state log,
  another asserts the transient prompt renders. Always compare a fixed run against a control zdot
  (same setup, feature under test disabled).
- Keep probes mode-aware: with zsh-vi-mode, a line after a normal-mode accept starts in normal
  mode - prefix typed commands with `i` where needed, and remember a stray self-inserted `i`
  turns the probe into a failing command (useful as a broken-state detector: `last_status=127`).

## Keeping a render alive

- There is no shell-command segment type. To hold `omp stream`/a render open for a controllable
  window, point an `http` segment at a silent local TCP listener
  (`python3 -c 'socket...accept...sleep'`) with a large `http_timeout`; kill the listener to
  trigger the async update record.

## Reading protocol streams

- One `bufio.Scanner` per pipe, ever - a second scanner on the same pipe loses buffered data
  (use a shared reader helper in Go tests).

content.content: Refer to `skills/golang/SKILL.md` for the full Go coding standards.
content.rawContent:
---
paths:
  - "**/*.go"

content.text:
Analysis is done — here's the landscape and the realistic options. No code written; this is the report-and-wait-for-go stage.

## Where things stand today

- The only automated shell coverage is unit tests in `src/shell/*_test.go` that assert the **generated init-script text** (e.g. [zsh_test.go](src/shell/zsh_test.go)). Nothing ever executes the script in a real shell, which is why regressions like the zsh `read -d`/`-t` hang or pwsh exit deadlocks only surface via issues.
- Ad-hoc harnesses already exist as knowledge, not code: the WSL zpty/`script(1)` patterns in [.agents/skills/project-knowledge/references/testing.md](.agents/skills/project-knowledge/references/testing.md) were built for real bug hunts (zsh streaming, vi-mode, fish streaming) and prove keystroke-level testing is feasible.
- Feature flags are derived from the config in [config.go:122](src/config/config.go:122) (`transient_prompt` present → `Transient`, tooltips array → `Tooltips`, rprompt block → `RPrompt`, …). So your idea of "a standard config with toggleable features" maps 1:1 onto how the product actually works — one base config plus per-scenario overlays is the natural test input.
- CI ([code.yml](.github/workflows/code.yml)) already runs a 3-OS matrix, so Windows-only surfaces (cmd+clink, Windows pwsh quirks, ConPTY) have a place to run.

The matrix to cover: 9 shells (bash, zsh, fish, pwsh, cmd/clink, nu, elvish, xonsh, yash) × 16 feature flags, though most features only apply to a few shells.

## What to test — a layered pyramid (driver-independent)

Whatever driver we pick, the suite should have layers, because they differ hugely in cost and flakiness:

1. **Static validation** — generate the init script for every feature combination and syntax-check it with the shell's own parser: `bash -n`, `zsh -n`, `fish --no-execute`, `nu-check`, `pwsh` parser, luacheck for clink. No pty, milliseconds per case, catches the most common regression class (broken embedded script) across the *full* feature matrix.
2. **Boot smoke** — start each shell interactively with the generated init in a pty, assert the first prompt renders, no error output, clean exit. This alone would have caught several past incidents (blank prompt from goroutine panic, exit deadlock).
3. **Behavioral scenarios** — per feature, drive keystrokes and assert outcomes: run `false` → status segment shows exit code (LineError); press Enter → transient prompt replaces the primary; RPrompt appears right-aligned; FTCS marks (OSC 133) present in the byte stream; tooltips fire on trigger word; vi-mode indicator flips on ESC; streaming/async updates arrive.
4. **Version matrix** (optional, nightly) — same suite inside Docker images pinning shell versions (zsh 5.8/5.9, fish 3.7/4.x, nu N/N-1 — nu breaks compatibility often). Linux shells only.

Layer 3 needs two assertion styles: raw byte-stream matching (escape sequences like FTCS/OSC) and *rendered screen* assertions (rprompt alignment, transient replacement, cursor positioning) — the latter needs either tmux's screen or a vt100 emulator.

## Driver options

**Option A — Go-native harness: `go test` + creack/pty (Unix) / ConPTY (Windows) + a vt10x-style terminal emulator.**
An `e2e` package behind a build tag; scenarios are table-driven Go tests parameterized by shell × feature overlay. Pros: one language (the repo's), runs on all three CI OSes including cmd/clink and Windows pwsh, full programmatic control, byte- and screen-level assertions via an in-process vt100 emulator. Cons: the most upfront work, especially ConPTY plumbing and per-shell readiness detection (each shell has pty quirks — the testing.md notes show bash/fish/zsh all behave differently).

**Option B — tmux harness.**
Spawn each shell in a tmux pane, `send-keys` to type, `capture-pane` to assert the rendered screen. Pros: screen-accurate assertions for free (rprompt, transient, cursor positioning are trivial to check), very little harness code, sidesteps most pty quirks uniformly. Cons: Unix only — no cmd, no Windows-specific pwsh behavior; adds a tmux dependency; timing-based synchronization needs care (poll for marker text, never sleep).

**Option C — pexpect/pytest suite.**
The approach fish itself uses for its own tests. Pros: mature expect-style API, pytest parameterization is ideal for a shell × feature matrix, very readable tests. Cons: introduces a Python toolchain to the repo, and Windows support (wexpect) is effectively unmaintained — cmd/clink coverage would still need something else.

**Option D — Docker per-shell images.**
Not a driver but a runtime layer: wraps A, B, or C to add shell-version pinning. Worth doing eventually for nu and fish major versions; Linux only.

## Determinism strategy (applies to all options)

- A standard base config using only deterministic segments: `text` templates reading env vars set by the harness, `status`, `session` with pinned env. No git/time/battery segments — or, once the `feature/template-data` branch lands, `--data` replay could make even rich segments deterministic.
- Feature toggles = JSON overlays merged onto the base config per scenario (add `transient_prompt`, add an rprompt block, add `tooltips`), since that's what actually flips the `Features` bits.
- Harness pins `TERM`, terminal size, `XDG_CACHE_HOME`/cache dir to a temp dir, and disables upgrade/notice so no network or cache bleed.

## Recommendation

Hybrid, built incrementally:

1. Start with **layer 1 (static syntax-check)** — cheap, immediate value, pure Go test code plus shells already present on CI runners.
2. Build **layer 2 + 3 on Option A (Go-native pty harness)** for the long term, because it's the only option covering Windows shells, and the repo stays single-language. Use tmux (Option B) only if we want screen assertions on Unix faster than a vt100 emulator integration; my take is the Go emulator route is worth the extra initial cost to avoid maintaining two harnesses.
3. Add **Docker version matrix (D)** later as a nightly job.

CI-wise: layers 1–2 on every PR (they're fast), layer 3 per-OS on PRs touching `src/shell` or `src/prompt`, full matrix nightly.

## Open questions before building

- Scope of shells for layer 3: all nine, or the big five (bash, zsh, fish, pwsh, nu) first with elvish/xonsh/yash at smoke-level only? I'd start with the big five.
- Should the harness live in `src/` (same module, build-tagged) or a separate `e2e/` module so its deps (pty, emulator) don't touch the main go.mod? I'd lean separate module.
- Do you want to wait for `template-data` replay to land for segment determinism, or start with env-driven text segments (my suggestion — don't couple the two)?

Give me a go (and your answers on the open questions, or I'll use my stated defaults) and I'll move to the planning phase.

file.content:
# Phase 2 — Plan

Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
must be able to execute each task without asking questions.

## Pin the spec

Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:

- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
  them.
- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
  compatibility requirements.
- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.

## Split into tasks

- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.

## Decide the workspace per task

- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
  and commit the result in the current session.
- Isolated worktree: everything else, especially parallel tasks — they must never share a
  working tree.

## Output of this phase

A task list where each entry names its executor tier (see Phase 3), its workspace, its
dependencies, and carries its pinned spec.

Then I'm looking at what configuration combinations are possible, including base configs with overlays like transient prompts, right-aligned prompt blocks, tooltips, and terminal feature flags.

For syntax validation, I'm considering the appropriate checkers for each shell: bash and zsh use their `-n` flags, fish uses `--no-execute`, nushell has a dedicated check command, and PowerShell uses its parser API. I'm also trying to clarify whether `oh-my-posh init --print` outputs the full initialization script or just a source directive, and whether there's caching involved that might serve stale scripts to development builds.

For the PTY harness and terminal emulation layer, I'm looking at cross-platform options: `aymanbagabas/go-pty` seems like the best choice since it wraps both Unix PTY and Windows ConPTY in a unified interface, and `charmbracelet/x/vt` for terminal emulation assertions. I'm also thinking about where to structure this—probably an `e2e/` directory with its own Go module, and whether CI integration is part of the initial scope. For the CI matrix, I'm thinking ubuntu-latest with all five shells is essential, and windows-latest with pwsh and maybe nu covers the other major platform. macOS could come later since bash 3.2 testing is valuable but adds cost. Locally on Windows ARM64, I can verify pwsh natively and potentially run linux tests through WSL where Go is already available, with the test harness skipping shells that aren't installed.

Now I'm laying out the e2e module structure with a top-level directory containing the harness that handles building or locating the omp binary, managing base configs with feature toggles, generating init scripts, handling PTY setup across platforms, managing terminal state with vt10x screen and wait helpers, and defining per-shell session logic for starting shells, typing commands, waiting for prompts, and capturing output.

The test suite is organized in three layers: syntax validation for each shell with their respective no-execute flags, smoke tests for basic functionality, and feature tests covering status, transient prompts, right prompts, tooltips, and combined scenarios.

For syntax checking, I'm handling PowerShell's parser through its language API to count errors, and for Nushell I need to wrap the nu-check command with proper exit codes. I also need to verify whether the init script generation produces the full output or just a source reference, as that affects how the tests validate the generated scripts.

For detecting when the prompt is ready, I'm using distinctive markers that each overlay type prints—like `OMPE2E>` for the base config, `RPROMPT-MARK` for right prompts, and `TP>` for transient prompts—then waiting for those markers to appear on screen.

Now I'm working through the environment setup: checking if Oh My Posh supports cache directory overrides via environment variables, disabling upgrade notices in the config, and then isolating each shell by controlling its startup files—bash gets `--norc --noprofile` with a custom rcfile, while zsh uses ZDOTDIR pointing to a temp directory containing just the init script.

For zsh specifically, I need to decide between using `zsh -f` to skip all rc files or combining ZDOTDIR with the `-d` flag to disable global rc files. Fish requires setting `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` to a temporary directory with a minimal config.fish, and PowerShell uses `-NoProfile` to skip its profile loading entirely, then passes the init command directly.

For Nushell, I'm checking whether the modern approach with `oh-my-posh init nu --print` can be sourced from the config file directly, since Nushell can't dynamically source paths but does accept a `--config` flag pointing to the config file.

Given how intricate these shell-specific details are, I'm thinking the best approach is to keep tasks coarse-grained and rely on verification loops to catch platform-specific gotchas. I'll delegate the scaffolding and core harness work to handle binary location, config overlays, and script generation with syntax validation, then tackle the PTY session harness separately with per-shell session support.

Before I lock in the spec, I need to verify a few critical facts about how the init command works and what environment variables are available for cache directory overrides.

I should also check which shells are available locally—PowerShell, Bash, Nushell, and WSL variants of Zsh and Fish. For the PTY library, I'm leaning toward aymanbagabas/go-pty since it handles cross-platform PTY with ConPTY support and is already used in charm's ecosystem, though I could fall back to creack/pty if needed. I'll also need vt10x for terminal emulation to capture output properly.
I'm moving forward with the big five using a separate module, and now I'll verify the technical foundations: how `init --print` works, cache-dir isolation, and local verification options.

1	package shell
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"os"
6		"strings"
7		"time"
8	
9		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/cache"
10		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/log"
11		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/runtime"
12		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/runtime/path"
13		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/text"
14	)
15	
16	const (
17		noExe = "echo \"Unable to find Oh My Posh executable\""
18	)
19	
20	var (
21		// identify ble.sh by validating the existence of BLE_SESSION_ID
22		bashBLEsession bool
23	)
24	
25	func getExecutablePath(env runtime.Environment) (string, error) {
26		executable, err := os.Executable()
27		if err != nil {
28			return "", err
29		}
30	
31		_, msix := cache.PackageFamilyName()
32		if msix || env.Flags().Strict {
33			return path.Base(executable), nil
34		}
35	
36		// On Windows, it fails when the executable is called in MSYS2 for example
37		// which uses unix style paths to resolve the executable's location.
38		// PowerShell knows how to resolve both, so we can swap this without any issue.
39		if env.GOOS() == runtime.WINDOWS {
40			executable = strings.ReplaceAll(executable, "\\", "/")
41		}
42	
43		return executable, nil
44	}
45	
46	// Init returns the command to initialize oh-my-posh for the shell.
47	// It writes the init script to the appropriate location and returns
48	// a source command or wrapper command depending on the shell.
49	// For Nu shell, it writes to the autoload directory and returns empty.
50	// For PWSH/Elvish, it returns a wrapper command that re-invokes oh-my-posh.
51	func Init(env runtime.Environment, feats Features) string {
52		switch env.Flags().Shell {
53		case PWSH:
54			if !env.Flags().Eval {
55				return generateAndSourceScript(env, feats)
56			}
57	
58			return recurseInitCommand(env)
59		case ELVISH:
60			return recurseInitCommand(env)
61		case NU:
62			return initNu(env, feats)
63		case ZSH, BASH, FISH, CMD, XONSH, YASH:
64			return generateAndSourceScript(env, feats)
65		default:
66			return fmt.Sprintf(`echo "%s is not supported by Oh My Posh"`, env.Flags().Shell)
67		}
68	}
69	
70	// Script returns the init script content directly.
71	// This is used by the --print flag to output the script to stdout.
72	func Script(env runtime.Environment, feats Features) string {
73		script := generateScript(env, feats)
74		return fmt.Sprintf("%s\n%s", sessionScript(env), script)
75	}
76	
77	// Debug writes the init script and returns debug information.
78	// This is used by the --debug flag.
79	func Debug(env runtime.Environment, feats Features, startTime *time.Time) string {
80		script := generateScript(env, feats)
81	
82		log.Debug(script)
83	
84		if _, err := writeScript(env, script); err != nil {
85			log.Error(err)
86		}
87	
88		return printDebugInfo(env, startTime)
89	}
90	
91	// recurseInitCommand returns a wrapper command that re-invokes oh-my-posh
92	// with --print. This is used by PWSH and Elvish which eval the script.
93	func recurseInitCommand(env runtime.Environment) string {
94		executable, err := getExecutablePath(env)
95		if err != nil {
96			return noExe
97		}
98	
99		var additionalParams string
100	
101		if env.Flags().Strict {
102			additionalParams += " --strict"
103		}
104	
105		if env.Flags().Eval {
106			additionalParams += " --eval"
107		}
108	
109		config := quotePwshOrElvishStr(env.Flags().ConfigPath)
110		executable = quotePwshOrElvishStr(executable)
111	
112		var command string
113	
114		switch env.Flags().Shell {
115		case PWSH:
116			command = "(@(& %s init %s --config=%s --print%s) -join \"`n\") | Invoke-Expression"
117		case ELVISH:
118			command = "eval ((external %s) init %s --config=%s --print%s | slurp)"
119		}
120	
121		return fmt.Sprintf(command, executable, env.Flags().Shell, config, additionalParams)
122	}
123	
124	// generateAndSourceScript writes the init script to the cache and returns a source command.
125	func generateAndSourceScript(env runtime.Environment, feats Features) string {
126		async := feats&Async != 0
127	
128		if scriptPath, ok := hasScript(env); ok {
129			return sourceCommand(env, scriptPath, async)
130		}
131	
132		script := generateScript(env, feats)
133	
134		log.Debug(script)
135	
136		scriptPath, err := writeScript(env, script)
137		if err != nil {
138			return fmt.Sprintf("echo \"Failed to write init script: %s\"", err.Error())
139		}
140	
141		return sourceCommand(env, scriptPath, async)
142	}
143	
144	// initNu writes the init script to Nu's autoload directory.
145	// It returns empty since Nu automatically loads from the autoload directory.
146	func initNu(env runtime.Environment, feats Features) string {
147		script := generateNuScript(env, feats)
148	
149		scriptPath, err := writeScript(env, script)
150		if err != nil {
151			return fmt.Sprintf("echo \"Failed to write init script: %s\"", err.Error())
152		}
153	
154		log.Debug("nu init script written to:", scriptPath)
155	
156		return ""
157	}
158	
159	// generateScript generates the init script content for the current shell.
160	func generateScript(env runtime.Environment, feats Features) string {
161		executable, err := getExecutablePath(env)
162		if err != nil {
163			return noExe
164		}
165	
166		bashBLEsession = len(env.Getenv("BLE_SESSION_ID")) != 0
167	
168		// Only nu consumes the ::CONFIG:: placeholder: it has no eval'd session
169		// script to export POSH_CONFIG from, so the value is baked into its init
170		// script instead. All other shells get it via sessionScript.
171		var config string
172	
173		var script string
174	
175		switch env.Flags().Shell {
176		case PWSH:
177			executable = quotePwshOrElvishStr(executable)
178			script = pwshInit
179		case ZSH:
180			executable = QuotePosixStr(executable)
181			script = zshInit
182		case BASH:
183			executable = QuotePosixStr(executable)
184			script = bashInit
185		case FISH:
186			executable = quoteFishStr(executable)
187			script = fishInit
188		case CMD:
189			executable = escapeLuaStr(executable)
190			script = cmdInit
191		case NU:
192			executable = quoteNuStr(executable)
193			config = quoteNuStr(env.Flags().ConfigPath)
194			script = nuInit
195		case ELVISH:
196			executable = quotePwshOrElvishStr(executable)
197			script = elvishInit
198		case XONSH:
199			executable = quotePythonStr(executable)
200			script = xonshInit
201		case YASH:
202			executable = quoteYashStr(executable)
203			script = yashInit
204		default:
205			return fmt.Sprintf("echo \"No initialization script available for %s\"", env.Flags().Shell)
206		}
207	
208		// Remove UTF-8 BOM if present, as it can cause issues in some shells.
209		script = strings.TrimPrefix(script, "\xef\xbb\xbf")
210	
211		init := strings.NewReplacer(
212			"::OMP::", executable,
213			"::SESSION_ID::", cache.SessionID(),
214			"::CONFIG::", config,
215		).Replace(script)
216	
217		return feats.Lines(env.Flags().Shell).String(init)
218	}
219	
220	// generateNuScript generates the init script content specifically for Nu shell.
221	func generateNuScript(env runtime.Environment, feats Features) string {
222		executable, err := getExecutablePath(env)
223		if err != nil {
224			return noExe
225		}
226	
227		executable = quoteNuStr(executable)
228	
229		init := strings.NewReplacer(
230			"::OMP::", executable,
231			"::SESSION_ID::", cache.SessionID(),
232			"::CONFIG::", quoteNuStr(env.Flags().ConfigPath),
233		).Replace(nuInit)
234	
235		return feats.Lines(NU).String(init)
236	}
237	
238	// sourceCommand returns the command to source the init script.
239	func sourceCommand(env runtime.Environment, scriptPath string, async bool) string {
240		if env.IsCygwin() {
241			var err error
242			scriptPath, err = env.RunCommand("cygpath", "-u", scriptPath)
243			if err != nil {
244				log.Error(err)
245				return fmt.Sprintf("echo \"Failed to convert Cygwin path due to %s\"", err.Error())
246			}
247		}
248	
249		script := sessionScript(env)
250	
251		if async {
252			return script + sourceCommandAsync(env.Flags().Shell, scriptPath)
253		}
254	
255		switch env.Flags().Shell {
256		case PWSH:
257			script += fmt.Sprintf("& %s", quotePwshOrElvishStr(scriptPath))
258		case ZSH, BASH:
259			script += fmt.Sprintf("source %s", QuotePosixStr(scriptPath))
260		case XONSH:
261			script += fmt.Sprintf("source %s", quotePythonStr(scriptPath))
262		case FISH:
263			script += fmt.Sprintf("source %s", quoteFishStr(scriptPath))
264		case YASH:
265			// yash has no source builtin, use the dot command instead
266			script += fmt.Sprintf(". %s", quoteYashStr(scriptPath))
267		case ELVISH:
268			script += fmt.Sprintf("eval (slurp < %s)", quotePwshOrElvishStr(scriptPath))
269		case CMD:
270			// dofile closes the file handle when done, io.open would leak it
271			// until the Lua GC kicks in, blocking script updates on Windows
272			script += fmt.Sprintf(`dofile('%s')`, escapeLuaStr(scriptPath))
273		default:
274			return fmt.Sprintf("echo \"No source command available for %s\"", env.Flags().Shell)
275		}
276	
277		return script
278	}
279	
280	// sourceCommandAsync returns the async source command for supported shells.
281	func sourceCommandAsync(shell, scriptPath string) string {
282		switch shell {
283		case PWSH:
284			return fmt.Sprintf("function prompt() { & %s }", quotePwshOrElvishStr(scriptPath))
285		case ZSH:
286			return fmt.Sprintf("precmd() { source %s }", QuotePosixStr(scriptPath))
287		case BASH:
288			command := fmt.Sprintf("source %s", QuotePosixStr(scriptPath))
289			return fmt.Sprintf("PROMPT_COMMAND=%s", QuotePosixStr(command))
290		case FISH:
291			return fmt.Sprintf("function fish_prompt; source %s; end", quoteFishStr(scriptPath))
292		default:
293			return ""
294		}
295	}
296	
297	func printDebugInfo(env runtime.Environment, startTime *time.Time) string {
298		builder := text.NewBuilder()
299	
300		builder.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("\n%s %s\n", log.Text("Init duration:").Green().Bold().Plain(), time.Since(*startTime)))
301	
302		builder.WriteString(log.Text("\n\nLogs:\n\n").Green().Bold().Plain().String())
303		builder.WriteString(env.Logs())
304	
305		return builder.String()
306	}
307	
308	// sessionScript exports the session's environment variables: POSH_SESSION_ID
309	// identifies the session cache, and POSH_CONFIG is pinned to the session's
310	// resolved configuration source so it can be recovered when the session cache
311	// is lost. A healthy session cache always wins, so POSH_CONFIG can not be used
312	// to change the configuration mid-session.
313	func sessionScript(env runtime.Environment) string {
314		sessionID := cache.SessionID()
315		config := env.Flags().ConfigPath
316	
317		switch env.Flags().Shell {
318		case PWSH:
319			return fmt.Sprintf("$env:POSH_SESSION_ID = \"%s\"; $env:POSH_CONFIG = %s;", sessionID, quotePwshOrElvishStr(config))
320		case ZSH, BASH:
321			return fmt.Sprintf("export POSH_SESSION_ID=\"%s\"; export POSH_CONFIG=%s;", sessionID, QuotePosixStr(config))
322		case YASH:
323			return fmt.Sprintf("export POSH_SESSION_ID=\"%s\"; export POSH_CONFIG=%s;", sessionID, quoteYashStr(config))
324		case XONSH:
325			return fmt.Sprintf("$POSH_SESSION_ID = \"%s\"; $POSH_CONFIG = %s;", sessionID, quotePythonStr(config))
326		case FISH:
327			return fmt.Sprintf("set --export --global POSH_SESSION_ID \"%s\"; set --export --global POSH_CONFIG %s;", sessionID, quoteFishStr(config))
328		case ELVISH:
329			return fmt.Sprintf("set-env POSH_SESSION_ID \"%s\"; set-env POSH_CONFIG %s;", sessionID, quotePwshOrElvishStr(config))
330		case CMD:
331			return fmt.Sprintf(`os.setenv('POSH_SESSION_ID', '%s'); os.setenv('POSH_CONFIG', '%s');`, sessionID, escapeLuaStr(config))
332		}
333		return ""
334	}
335

content.thinking:
I'm noticing some inconsistency in the architecture detection — the local machine reports x86_64 through `uname -m`, but WSL indicates aarch64, which aligns with the ARM64 memory. Git Bash is likely running x64 emulation on ARM. I have access to multiple shells across both Windows and WSL: PowerShell and Nu on Windows, plus Bash and Git Bash, while WSL provides Zsh, Fish, and Bash running natively on aarch64 with Fish 4.1 and Zsh 5.9. All five major shells are available locally for testing.

Looking at the init.go implementation, the `--print` flag outputs the complete script content through `shell.Script()`, prefixed with environment exports for syntax validation. This works across all shells — Nu generates the full script with config placeholders, CMD uses Lua (clink), and PowerShell outputs a complete ps1 script. The normal init flow (without `--print`) caches the script and returns a source command instead.

For layer 2/3 testing, I could either isolate the cache directory using environment variables like `XDG_CACHE_HOME` or `LOCALAPPDATA`, or bypass caching entirely by using `--print` and directly evaluating the output. The `--print` approach works for bash/zsh/fish with `eval "$(omp init <shell> --config c --print)"`, though I need to verify how PowerShell handles this. For nu, the autoload setup writes to the vendor directory under the nu data path, but the harness can simplify this by using --print to output the initialization script and sourcing it from a minimal config file, with XDG_DATA_HOME controlling the data directory on Linux. I'll build flexibility into the test spec to handle per-shell initialization strategies differently based on what each shell's documented setup requires.

Now I need to figure out how the cache directory gets resolved across these different shells.
I've confirmed that `--print` outputs the full script and verified all five shells work locally. Now I'm checking cache-dir isolation and the `--print` CLI path.
