43	}
44	
45	// Start launches sh interactively with cfgPath as its oh-my-posh config, in a 120x30 pty.
46	// It skips the test if sh's binary is missing or unsupported on the current platform,
47	// otherwise it fails the test on any setup error. The returned Session's process and pty
48	// are killed and closed via t.Cleanup.
49	func Start(t *testing.T, sh ShellDef, cfgPath string) *Session {
50		t.Helper()
51	
52		if !sh.SupportedOnHost() {
53			t.Skipf("%s is not supported on this platform, skipping", sh.Name)
54		}
55	
56		binPath, err := LookupShellBinary(sh.Binary)
57		if err != nil {
58			t.Skipf("%s not found on PATH, skipping", sh.Binary)
59		}
60	
61		workDir := t.TempDir()
62		script := InitScript(t, sh.Name, cfgPath)
63		scriptPath := WriteScript(t, sh.Name, script)
64	
65		_, args, env := sh.Launch(t, scriptPath, workDir)
66		env = append(env, "TERM=xterm-256color", "OMP_CACHE_DIR="+t.TempDir())
67	
68		p, err := pty.New()
69		if err != nil {
70			t.Fatalf("creating pty: %v", err)
71		}
72	
73		if err := p.Resize(ptyCols, ptyRows); err != nil {
74			_ = p.Close()
75			t.Fatalf("resizing pty: %v", err)
76		}
77	
78		cmd := p.Command(binPath, args...)
79		cmd.Dir = workDir
80		cmd.Env = env
81	
82		if err := cmd.Start(); err != nil {
83			_ = p.Close()
84			t.Fatalf("starting %s: %v", sh.Name, err)
85		}
86	
87		s := &Session{
88			t:    t,
89			pty:  p,
90			cmd:  cmd,
91			term: vt10x.New(vt10x.WithSize(ptyCols, ptyRows)),
92		}
93	
94		go s.read()
95	
96		t.Cleanup(func() {
97			_ = cmd.Process.Kill()
98			_ = p.Close()
99		})
100	
101		return s
102	}
103	
104	// dsrRequest is the Device Status Report cursor-position query (CSI 6n). PSReadLine on
105	// Linux issues it repeatedly and blocks on the reply; ConPTY answers it internally on
106	// Windows, but on a raw Unix pty the "terminal" — this harness — must respond itself.
107	var dsrRequest = []byte("\x1b[6n")
108	
109	// read copies pty output into both the vt10x terminal and the raw buffer until the pty is
110	// closed, answering DSR cursor-position queries along the way. It is the session's single
111	// reader goroutine.
112	func (s *Session) read() {
113		buf := make([]byte, 4096)
114	
115		// carry holds the last len(dsrRequest)-1 bytes of the previous chunk so a query split
116		// across two reads is still detected; a carry shorter than the full sequence can never
117		// double count.
118		var carry []byte
119	
120		for {
121			n, err := s.pty.Read(buf)
122			if n > 0 {
123				s.mu.Lock()
124				s.raw.Write(buf[:n])
125				_, _ = s.term.Write(buf[:n])
126				cursor := s.term.Cursor()
127				s.mu.Unlock()
128	
129				window := append(carry, buf[:n]...)
130				requests := bytes.Count(window, dsrRequest)
131	
132				if tailLen := min(len(window), len(dsrRequest)-1); tailLen > 0 {
133					carry = append(carry[:0], window[len(window)-tailLen:]...)
134				}
135	
136				for range requests {
137					s.writeMu.Lock()
138					_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(s.pty, "\x1b[%d;%dR", cursor.Y+1, cursor.X+1)
139					s.writeMu.Unlock()
140				}
141			}
142	
143			if err != nil {
144				return
145			}
146		}
147	}
148	
149	// Screen returns the current rendered terminal screen as text.
150	func (s *Session) Screen() string {
151		s.mu.Lock()
152		defer s.mu.Unlock()
153	
154		return s.term.String()
155	}
156	
157	// ScreenLines returns the current rendered terminal screen split into its individual
158	// rows, each padded with trailing spaces to the pty's column width. Use this over Screen
159	// when an assertion needs to reason about a single row (e.g. what shares a line with the
160	// prompt, or how far right some text is rendered).
161	func (s *Session) ScreenLines() []string {
162		return strings.Split(s.Screen(), "\n")
163	}
164	
165	// Raw returns the raw bytes captured from the pty so far, as a string.
166	func (s *Session) Raw() string {
167		s.mu.Lock()
168		defer s.mu.Unlock()
169	
170		return s.raw.String()
171	}
172	
173	// WaitFor polls Screen every 50ms until it matches re, up to a 30s deadline. It returns
174	// the matching screen text, or fails the test with the full rendered screen and the tail
175	// of the raw output if the deadline elapses first.
176	func (s *Session) WaitFor(re *regexp.Regexp) string {
177		s.t.Helper()
178	
179		deadline := time.Now().Add(waitForDeadline)
180	
181		for time.Now().Before(deadline) {
182			screen := s.Screen()
183			if re.MatchString(screen) {
184				return screen
185			}
186	
187			time.Sleep(waitForPollInterval)
188		}
189	
190		s.t.Fatalf("timed out waiting for %q\n--- screen ---\n%s\n--- raw tail ---\n%s",
191			re.String(), s.Screen(), tail(s.Raw(), 2000))
192	
193		return ""
194	}
195	
196	// tail returns the last n bytes of s, or s unchanged if it is shorter than n.
197	func tail(s string, n int) string {
198		if len(s) <= n {
199			return s
200		}
201	
202		return s[len(s)-n:]
203	}
204	
205	// WaitForPrompt waits for the "E2E:<code>>" prompt marker to appear on screen.
206	func (s *Session) WaitForPrompt() string {
207		return s.WaitFor(promptRegexp)
208	}
209	
210	// SendLine writes cmd followed by a carriage return to the pty, as if typed interactively.
211	func (s *Session) SendLine(cmd string) {
212		s.writeMu.Lock()
213		defer s.writeMu.Unlock()
214	
215		_, _ = s.pty.Write([]byte(cmd + "\r"))
216	}
217	
218	// ExpectExit sends "exit\r" and waits up to 15s for the process to terminate. It fails the
219	// test (killing the process first) if the process does not exit in time.
220	func (s *Session) ExpectExit() {
221		s.t.Helper()
222	
223		s.SendLine("exit")
224	
225		done := make(chan error, 1)
226		go func() { done <- s.cmd.Wait() }()
227	
228		select {
229		case <-done:
230			return
231		case <-time.After(exitDeadline):
232			_ = s.cmd.Process.Kill()
233			s.t.Fatalf("shell did not exit within %s\n--- screen ---\n%s", exitDeadline, s.Screen())
234		}
235	}

content.content:
1	package e2e
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"regexp"
6		"strings"
7		"testing"
8	
9		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/e2e/harness"
10		"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
11		"github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
12	)
13	
14	// featurePtyCols mirrors the pty width the harness fixes every session to (see
15	// harness/session.go), used here to assert how close to the right edge the rprompt
16	// renders.
17	const featurePtyCols = 120
18	
19	// TestExitCode boots every shell with the base config, runs its ShellDef.Fail command,
20	// and asserts the next prompt reports the expected exit code via the "E2E:<code>>"
21	// template. Shells whose binary is missing or that are unsupported on this platform skip
22	// cleanly via harness.Start.
23	func TestExitCode(t *testing.T) {
24		for _, sh := range harness.Shells {
25			t.Run(sh.Name, func(t *testing.T) {
26				require.NotEmpty(t, sh.Fail.Command, "%s: no Fail command configured", sh.Name)
27	
28				cfgPath := harness.WriteConfig(t)
29				session := harness.Start(t, sh, cfgPath)
30	
31				session.WaitForPrompt()
32				session.SendLine(sh.Fail.Command)
33	
34				expected := fmt.Sprintf("E2E:%d>", sh.Fail.Code)
35				session.WaitFor(regexp.MustCompile(regexp.QuoteMeta(expected)))
36			})
37		}
38	}
39	
40	// TestTransient boots every shell with the transient_prompt overlay, types a command, and
41	// asserts that once the command's output and the following prompt have both landed, the
42	// screen row that carried the accepted command line was rewritten to start with "TR>" —
43	// i.e. the primary prompt on that line was replaced by the transient one.
44	func TestTransient(t *testing.T) {
45		const echoedText = "transient-check"
46	
47		for _, sh := range harness.Shells {
48			t.Run(sh.Name, func(t *testing.T) {
49				cfgPath := harness.WriteConfig(t, harness.Transient)
50				session := harness.Start(t, sh, cfgPath)
51	
52				session.WaitForPrompt()
53				session.SendLine("echo " + echoedText)
54	
55				// (?s) lets '.' cross line boundaries: this only matches once the echoed
56				// output AND a subsequent primary prompt are both on screen, proving the
57				// command has fully run and the shell moved on to its next prompt.
58				readyRe := regexp.MustCompile(`(?s)` + echoedText + `.*E2E:\d+>`)
59				screen := session.WaitFor(readyRe)
60	
61				var commandLine string
62				for _, line := range session.ScreenLines() {
63					if strings.Contains(line, "echo "+echoedText) {
64						commandLine = line
65						break
66					}
67				}
68	
69				require.NotEmpty(t, commandLine,
70					"%s: could not find the accepted command line on screen:\n%s", sh.Name, screen)
71	
72				trimmed := strings.TrimLeft(commandLine, " ")
73				assert.True(t, strings.HasPrefix(trimmed, "TR>"),
74					"%s: command line was not rewritten with the transient prompt: %q", sh.Name, commandLine)
75			})
76		}
77	}
78	
79	// TestRPrompt boots every shell with the rprompt overlay and asserts the fixed "RMARK"
80	// marker renders on the same screen row as the primary "E2E:0>" prompt, right-aligned
81	// near the pty's 120th column.
82	//
83	// bash only renders a right prompt inside a ble.sh session (gated on the BLE_SESSION_ID
84	// environment variable, see src/shell/bash.go), which this harness's plain
85	// `bash --noprofile --rcfile ... -i` session does not provide, so bash is skipped here
86	// explicitly rather than asserted against.
87	func TestRPrompt(t *testing.T) {
88		for _, sh := range harness.Shells {
89			t.Run(sh.Name, func(t *testing.T) {
90				if sh.Name == "bash" {
91					t.Skip("bash only renders a right prompt inside a ble.sh session; not supported by this harness")
92				}
93	
94				cfgPath := harness.WriteConfig(t, harness.RPrompt)
95				session := harness.Start(t, sh, cfgPath)
96	
97				screen := session.WaitForPrompt()
98	
99				var promptLine string
100				for _, line := range session.ScreenLines() {
101					if strings.Contains(line, "E2E:0>") {
102						promptLine = line
103						break
104					}
105				}
106	
107				require.NotEmpty(t, promptLine,
108					"%s: could not find the primary prompt row on screen:\n%s", sh.Name, screen)
109	
110				require.Contains(t, promptLine, "RMARK",
111					"%s: RMARK not found on the same row as the primary prompt: %q", sh.Name, promptLine)
112	
113				trimmed := strings.TrimRight(promptLine, " ")
114				assert.True(t, strings.HasSuffix(trimmed, "RMARK"),
115					"%s: RMARK is not right-aligned, trimmed row does not end with it: %q", sh.Name, promptLine)
116	
117				endCol := strings.LastIndex(promptLine, "RMARK") + len("RMARK")
118				assert.InDelta(t, featurePtyCols, endCol, 2,
119					"%s: RMARK does not end near column %d (ended at %d): %q", sh.Name, featurePtyCols, endCol, promptLine)
120			})
121		}
122	}
123

content.thinking: There's a potential race condition with TestRPrompt—it waits for the primary prompt to match and immediately reads screen lines, but the right prompt marker might not have rendered yet depending on the shell (since shells like nu, fish, and zsh handle right prompts differently, some rendering them as separate hooks). It'd be safer to wait for a regex that accounts for this timing issue.

   - Concurrency group like code.yml's.
   - Job `linux` on ubuntu-latest: checkout (pin the same actions/checkout SHA code.yml uses), Go via the composite `./.github/workflows/composite/bootstrap-go` (note: its cache-dependency-path is src/go.sum — pass or add e2e/go.sum handling ONLY if the composite supports inputs; otherwise accept the default and do not modify the composite), install shells: `sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y zsh fish`, install nushell by downloading a pinned release tarball from GitHub releases (x86_64-unknown-linux-musl or -gnu; pick a current version, put the version in an env var at the top of the job, add the binary to GITHUB_PATH), then `cd e2e && go test -count=1 -v ./...`. pwsh and bash are preinstalled on ubuntu-latest.
   - Job `windows` on windows-latest: checkout, composite Go bootstrap, install nushell (pinned release zip for x86_64-pc-windows-msvc, added to GITHUB_PATH — same env-var version), then `cd e2e && go test -count=1 -v ./...`. pwsh is preinstalled; bash/zsh/fish skip on Windows by design.

content.text:
Task complete. Summary:

## Files created

- `D:\oh-my-posh\.github\workflows\e2e.yml` — new PR-triggered workflow with `linux` and `windows` jobs.
- `D:\oh-my-posh\e2e\README.md` — developer doc for the suite.

No files under `src/` or `e2e/*.go` were touched.

## Workflow details

- **Trigger**: `pull_request` limited to `paths: src/**, e2e/**, .github/workflows/e2e.yml`.
- **Concurrency**: same group expression as `code.yml`.
- **Both jobs**: checkout pinned to `actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0` (same SHA used everywhere else in the repo) and the composite `./.github/workflows/composite/bootstrap-go` unmodified (its `cache-dependency-path: src/go.sum` is hardcoded with no inputs, so per your instruction I left it as-is — it just means the e2e module's own deps aren't covered by the Go module cache key, which only affects cache-hit rate, not correctness).
- **linux** (ubuntu-latest): `sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y zsh fish`, then downloads the pinned nu tarball and adds the extracted dir to `GITHUB_PATH`, then `working-directory: e2e` / `go test -count=1 -v ./...`.
- **windows** (windows-latest): downloads the pinned nu zip via `Invoke-WebRequest`, expands it, adds it to `GITHUB_PATH`, then the same `go test` step.

## Nushell version: 0.113.1

Chosen instead of the newest release (0.114.1, published 5 days before "today") because **0.113.1 is what's installed on this dev machine**, and it's the exact version the harness's `nu-check`/`--config`/`--env-config`/caret-external syntax was spike-verified against in tasks 1–3. Pinning CI to a version I haven't personally exercised risked a CLI-flag mismatch; pinning to the verified version doesn't. I confirmed via `gh api repos/nushell/nushell/releases/tags/0.113.1` that its asset names follow the expected pattern (`nu-0.113.1-x86_64-unknown-linux-musl.tar.gz`, `nu-0.113.1-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip`), and downloaded both to verify archive layout: the Linux tarball extracts into a `nu-0.113.1-x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/` subdirectory (added to `GITHUB_PATH`), the Windows zip extracts flat with `nu.exe` at top level (extracted into a `nu\` dir, that dir added to `GITHUB_PATH`). Chose musl over gnu for the Linux binary to avoid any glibc-version coupling to the runner image.

I also checked `actions/runner-images` readmes for `ubuntu-24.04` and `windows-2025` (current `ubuntu-latest`/`windows-latest`) to confirm bash + PowerShell 7.6.3 are preinstalled on Ubuntu and PowerShell 7.6.3 is preinstalled on Windows, with no zsh/fish entries on either — matching the task's assumptions and justifying the `apt-get install` step.

## Validation performed

- **YAML**: parsed with PyYAML (`yaml.safe_load`) via a scratch script; printed the full jobs/steps structure to confirm shape. `actionlint` was not available on this machine (checked, not installed), so I relied on the PyYAML parse plus manual line-by-line review against `code.yml`'s conventions (indentation, checkout SHA, composite usage). **The workflow is untested in CI** — no PR has actually run it.
- **Markdown**: `npx markdownlint-cli2 e2e/README.md` — initially failed on `MD060/table-column-style` (compact/aligned pipe spacing in the shell-support table); fixed by generating the table programmatically to guarantee exact column alignment. Final run: `0 error(s)`.
- **Go tests**: `cd e2e && go test -count=1 ./...` → `ok github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/e2e 11.372s` (harness package: no test files, expected). Confirms the doc/workflow-only changes didn't break anything.
- `git status` confirms only `.github/workflows/e2e.yml` and the `e2e/` directory (including the pre-existing task 1–3 files) are untracked/new — nothing under `src/` was touched.

## Ambiguities encountered (resolved, noted here rather than left silent)

- **README H1 heading**: the markdown skill says not to use an H1 since "this will be generated based on the title" — true for website `.mdx` docs, but the repo's other non-website READMEs (e.g. `packages/msix/README.md`) do use an H1, and this file isn't part of the Docusaurus pipeline. I followed the `packages/msix/README.md` precedent and kept an H1 (`# E2E Test Suite`); markdownlint raised no objection.
- **`go test` invocation style**: the spec said literally `cd e2e && go test ...`; I used `working-directory: e2e` with a plain `go test` command instead, which is functionally identical and matches the `defaults.run.working-directory` idiom already used in `code.yml`/`gomod.yml`. Flagging in case you wanted the literal `cd &&` form for some other reason.

Everything else matched the spec directly; nothing else required improvisation.

file.content:
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/**'
      - 'e2e/**'
      - '.github/workflows/e2e.yml'

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

name: E2E Tests
jobs:
  linux:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      NU_VERSION: "0.113.1"
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0
    - name: Install Go 🗳
      uses: ./.github/workflows/composite/bootstrap-go
    - name: Install zsh and fish
      run: |
        sudo apt-get update
        sudo apt-get install -y zsh fish
    - name: Install nushell
      run: |
        curl -sSL -o nu.tar.gz "https://github.com/nushell/nushell/releases/download/${NU_VERSION}/nu-${NU_VERSION}-x86_64-unknown-linux-musl.tar.gz"
        tar xzf nu.tar.gz
        echo "$PWD/nu-${NU_VERSION}-x86_64-unknown-linux-musl" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"
    - name: E2E tests
      working-directory: e2e
      run: go test -count=1 -v ./...

  windows:
    runs-on: windows-latest
    env:
      NU_VERSION: "0.113.1"
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0
    - name: Install Go 🗳
      uses: ./.github/workflows/composite/bootstrap-go
    - name: Install nushell
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://github.com/nushell/nushell/releases/download/$env:NU_VERSION/nu-$env:NU_VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip" -OutFile nu.zip
        Expand-Archive -Path nu.zip -DestinationPath nu
        Add-Content -Path $env:GITHUB_PATH -Value "$PWD\nu"
    - name: E2E tests
      working-directory: e2e
      run: go test -count=1 -v ./...

content.content:
1	# E2E Test Suite
2	
3	End-to-end tests for the oh-my-posh shell integrations themselves (not the Go internals under
4	`src/`). They generate real init scripts with the `oh-my-posh` binary, feed them to the actual
5	shells, and drive interactive sessions in a pseudo-terminal.
6	
7	This is a separate Go module (`github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/e2e`, own `go.mod`/`go.sum`)
8	so its dependencies never leak into `src/`.
9	
10	## Layers
11	
12	1. **Syntax** (`syntax_test.go`) — generate the init script per shell x config overlay and
13	   validate it with the shell's own parser (`bash -n`, `zsh -n`, `fish --no-execute`, a
14	   `System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser` call for pwsh, `nu-check` for nu).
15	2. **Smoke** (`smoke_test.go`) — boot each shell interactively in a real pty with the generated
16	   init script, assert the prompt renders cleanly, a typed command runs, and the shell exits.
17	3. **Behavior** (`features_test.go`) — per-feature scenarios: exit-code propagation, transient
18	   prompt, right prompt.
19	
20	Both layers 2 and 3 drive the shell through `harness.Session`, a pty wrapper with a vt10x screen
21	emulator for rendered-screen assertions and a raw-byte buffer for escape-sequence assertions.
22	
23	The suite targets the "big five" shells: bash, zsh, fish, pwsh, nu.
24	
25	## Running locally
26	
27	```shell
28	cd e2e
29	go test -count=1 ./...
30	```
31	
32	Add `-v` for per-shell/per-overlay output, or `-run <Test>/<shell>` to scope to one case.
33	
34	Every test skips cleanly (`t.Skip`) when a shell's binary is not on `PATH`, or when the shell is
35	not supported on the current platform:
36	
37	| Shell | Linux/macOS                  | Windows                                                    |
38	|-------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
39	| bash  | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped (msys/WSL bash under ConPTY is not representative) |
40	| zsh   | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped                                                    |
41	| fish  | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped                                                    |
42	| pwsh  | yes (skip if binary missing) | yes (skip if binary missing)                               |
43	| nu    | yes (skip if binary missing) | yes (skip if binary missing)                               |
44	
45	Layer 1 (syntax) still runs its non-empty/no-placeholder assertions unconditionally for every
46	shell; only the parser check itself is skipped when the binary is missing.
47	
48	### Building the omp binary
49	
50	The suite builds `../src` once per test run (guarded by `sync.Once`) into a temp directory and
51	uses that binary for every `init` invocation. Set `OMP_E2E_BINARY` to an absolute path to skip
52	the build and reuse a prebuilt binary instead — useful when iterating on tests without rebuilding
53	oh-my-posh every run.
54	
55	### Isolation
56	
57	Every omp invocation and shell session gets `OMP_CACHE_DIR` pointed at a fresh `t.TempDir()`, so
58	runs never share or pollute a developer's real cache. Sessions also fix `TERM=xterm-256color`
59	and a 120x30 pty size.
60	
61	## Adding a new shell
62	
63	Add an entry to the `Shells` table in `harness/shells.go`. A `ShellDef` needs:
64	
65	- `Name` — the omp shell name passed to `oh-my-posh init <Name>`.
66	- `Binary` — the executable looked up via `LookupShellBinary`; missing means skip.
67	- `SyntaxCheck(scriptPath) *exec.Cmd` — a command that parses (not executes) the script and
68	  exits non-zero on a syntax error.
69	- `Launch(t, scriptPath, workDir) (bin string, args []string, env []string)` — how to boot the
70	  shell interactively with `scriptPath` sourced (e.g. a temp rc file plus `-i`).
71	- `Fail` — an `ExitCommand{Command, Code}`: a command line that reliably exits non-zero when
72	  typed interactively, and the exit code oh-my-posh should report in the next prompt.
73	
74	If the shell can't be driven faithfully on every platform, add a case to
75	`ShellDef.SupportedOnHost` (see the bash/zsh/fish Windows exclusion for the reasoning).
76	
77	Layers 1-3 all iterate `harness.Shells`, so a correctly filled-in entry is picked up everywhere
78	automatically — no test file needs editing for a new shell on its own.
79	
80	## Adding a new feature scenario
81	
82	1. Add an `Overlay` function to `harness/config.go` that mutates the base config map (see
83	   `Transient`, `RPrompt`, `Tooltips` for examples), and register it in `overlaySets` in
84	   `syntax_test.go` so layer 1 covers the new overlay's generated script.
85	2. Add a test (or a case in an existing `Test*` function) in `features_test.go`:
86	   - build the config with `harness.WriteConfig(t, harness.YourOverlay)`
87	   - `harness.Start(t, sh, cfgPath)` and `WaitForPrompt()`
88	   - drive the session (`SendLine`, `WaitFor`) and assert against `Screen()`/`ScreenLines()`
89	3. Skip shells that don't support the feature explicitly, with a comment explaining why (see the
90	   bash skips in `TestTransient`/`TestRPrompt` for the pattern), rather than silently succeeding
91	   or failing.
92	
93	## Known limitations
94	
95	- bash only renders a transient prompt or right prompt inside a `ble.sh` session (gated on
96	  `BLE_SESSION_ID`, see `src/shell/bash.go`); this harness's plain
97	  `bash --noprofile --rcfile ... -i` session doesn't provide one, so `TestTransient` and
98	  `TestRPrompt` skip bash explicitly.
99	- cmd, elvish, xonsh and yash are not covered by any layer.
100	
101	## CI
102	
103	`.github/workflows/e2e.yml` runs this suite on `ubuntu-latest` (bash, pwsh preinstalled; zsh and
104	fish installed via `apt`; nu installed from a pinned GitHub release) and on `windows-latest`

file.content:
# E2E Test Suite

End-to-end tests for the oh-my-posh shell integrations themselves (not the Go internals under
`src/`). They generate real init scripts with the `oh-my-posh` binary, feed them to the actual
shells, and drive interactive sessions in a pseudo-terminal.

This is a separate Go module (`github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/e2e`, own `go.mod`/`go.sum`)
so its dependencies never leak into `src/`.

## Layers

1. **Syntax** (`syntax_test.go`) — generate the init script per shell x config overlay and
   validate it with the shell's own parser (`bash -n`, `zsh -n`, `fish --no-execute`, a
   `System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser` call for pwsh, `nu-check` for nu).
2. **Smoke** (`smoke_test.go`) — boot each shell interactively in a real pty with the generated
   init script, assert the prompt renders cleanly, a typed command runs, and the shell exits.
3. **Behavior** (`features_test.go`) — per-feature scenarios: exit-code propagation, transient
   prompt, right prompt.

Both layers 2 and 3 drive the shell through `harness.Session`, a pty wrapper with a vt10x screen
emulator for rendered-screen assertions and a raw-byte buffer for escape-sequence assertions.

The suite targets the "big five" shells: bash, zsh, fish, pwsh, nu.

## Running locally

```shell
cd e2e
go test -count=1 ./...
```

Add `-v` for per-shell/per-overlay output, or `-run <Test>/<shell>` to scope to one case.

Every test skips cleanly (`t.Skip`) when a shell's binary is not on `PATH`, or when the shell is
not supported on the current platform:

| Shell | Linux/macOS                  | Windows                                                    |
|-------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| bash  | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped (msys/WSL bash under ConPTY is not representative) |
| zsh   | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped                                                    |
| fish  | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped                                                    |
| pwsh  | yes (skip if binary missing) | yes (skip if binary missing)                               |
| nu    | yes (skip if binary missing) | yes (skip if binary missing)                               |

Layer 1 (syntax) still runs its non-empty/no-placeholder assertions unconditionally for every
shell; only the parser check itself is skipped when the binary is missing.

### Building the omp binary

The suite builds `../src` once per test run (guarded by `sync.Once`) into a temp directory and
uses that binary for every `init` invocation. Set `OMP_E2E_BINARY` to an absolute path to skip
the build and reuse a prebuilt binary instead — useful when iterating on tests without rebuilding
oh-my-posh every run.

### Isolation

Every omp invocation and shell session gets `OMP_CACHE_DIR` pointed at a fresh `t.TempDir()`, so
runs never share or pollute a developer's real cache. Sessions also fix `TERM=xterm-256color`
and a 120x30 pty size.

## Adding a new shell

Add an entry to the `Shells` table in `harness/shells.go`. A `ShellDef` needs:

- `Name` — the omp shell name passed to `oh-my-posh init <Name>`.
- `Binary` — the executable looked up via `LookupShellBinary`; missing means skip.
- `SyntaxCheck(scriptPath) *exec.Cmd` — a command that parses (not executes) the script and
  exits non-zero on a syntax error.
- `Launch(t, scriptPath, workDir) (bin string, args []string, env []string)` — how to boot the
  shell interactively with `scriptPath` sourced (e.g. a temp rc file plus `-i`).
- `Fail` — an `ExitCommand{Command, Code}`: a command line that reliably exits non-zero when
  typed interactively, and the exit code oh-my-posh should report in the next prompt.

If the shell can't be driven faithfully on every platform, add a case to
`ShellDef.SupportedOnHost` (see the bash/zsh/fish Windows exclusion for the reasoning).

Layers 1-3 all iterate `harness.Shells`, so a correctly filled-in entry is picked up everywhere
automatically — no test file needs editing for a new shell on its own.

## Adding a new feature scenario

1. Add an `Overlay` function to `harness/config.go` that mutates the base config map (see
   `Transient`, `RPrompt`, `Tooltips` for examples), and register it in `overlaySets` in
   `syntax_test.go` so layer 1 covers the new overlay's generated script.
2. Add a test (or a case in an existing `Test*` function) in `features_test.go`:
   - build the config with `harness.WriteConfig(t, harness.YourOverlay)`
   - `harness.Start(t, sh, cfgPath)` and `WaitForPrompt()`
   - drive the session (`SendLine`, `WaitFor`) and assert against `Screen()`/`ScreenLines()`
3. Skip shells that don't support the feature explicitly, with a comment explaining why (see the
   bash skips in `TestTransient`/`TestRPrompt` for the pattern), rather than silently succeeding
   or failing.

## Known limitations

- bash only renders a transient prompt or right prompt inside a `ble.sh` session (gated on
  `BLE_SESSION_ID`, see `src/shell/bash.go`); this harness's plain
  `bash --noprofile --rcfile ... -i` session doesn't provide one, so `TestTransient` and
  `TestRPrompt` skip bash explicitly.
- cmd, elvish, xonsh and yash are not covered by any layer.

## CI

`.github/workflows/e2e.yml` runs this suite on `ubuntu-latest` (bash, pwsh preinstalled; zsh and
fish installed via `apt`; nu installed from a pinned GitHub release) and on `windows-latest`
(pwsh preinstalled; nu installed from a pinned release; bash/zsh/fish skip by design).

1	# Phase 5 — Verify
2	
3	Verification belongs to the orchestrator and runs on the final, merged state of the change.
4	It has two halves: the project's quality gates, and functional proof.
5	
6	## Quality gates
7	
8	- Build, full test suite, formatters, and linters — all must pass with zero errors.
9	- Apply the project's language skill when one exists (for example the Go skill's pre-commit
10	  gate: `modernize`, `fieldalignment`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt`, `golangci-lint`).
11	- Cross-compile when platform-specific files changed (`_windows.go`, `_unix.go`, and the like) —
12	  the local OS linter skips the other platform's rules.
13	
14	## Functional proof
15	
16	Tests passing is necessary, not sufficient. Run the real flow and confirm concrete outputs:
17	render the prompt, execute the command, hit the endpoint. Record the actual values observed —
18	the final report quotes them as evidence, not adjectives.
19	
20	When the user said they will do the manual validation, state exactly what they should check and
21	what the expected result is.
22	
23	## Documentation
24	
25	Documentation changes ship in the same change as the code they describe. Check for every
26	user-visible behavior change:
27	
28	- Project docs / website pages for the touched feature.
29	- README or setup instructions when flags, commands, or defaults changed.
30	
31	## On failure
32	
33	A failed gate or a wrong functional result sends the task back to Phase 4 (fix via the
34	implementer) or Phase 1 (the analysis was wrong). Never weaken a gate, skip a linter, or delete
35	a test to get to green.

file.content:
# Phase 5 — Verify

Verification belongs to the orchestrator and runs on the final, merged state of the change.
It has two halves: the project's quality gates, and functional proof.

## Quality gates

- Build, full test suite, formatters, and linters — all must pass with zero errors.
- Apply the project's language skill when one exists (for example the Go skill's pre-commit
  gate: `modernize`, `fieldalignment`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt`, `golangci-lint`).
- Cross-compile when platform-specific files changed (`_windows.go`, `_unix.go`, and the like) —
  the local OS linter skips the other platform's rules.

## Functional proof

Tests passing is necessary, not sufficient. Run the real flow and confirm concrete outputs:
render the prompt, execute the command, hit the endpoint. Record the actual values observed —
the final report quotes them as evidence, not adjectives.

When the user said they will do the manual validation, state exactly what they should check and
what the expected result is.

## Documentation

Documentation changes ship in the same change as the code they describe. Check for every
user-visible behavior change:

- Project docs / website pages for the touched feature.
- README or setup instructions when flags, commands, or defaults changed.

## On failure

A failed gate or a wrong functional result sends the task back to Phase 4 (fix via the
implementer) or Phase 1 (the analysis was wrong). Never weaken a gate, skip a linter, or delete
a test to get to green.

input.new_string:
# Testing shells end-to-end

Patterns for functionally driving omp's shell integrations, mostly in WSL (verified on aarch64,
zsh 5.9, fish 4.1.2).

## The e2e module (`e2e/`)

A separate Go module with a cross-platform pty harness (go-pty + vt10x) that runs three layers —
syntax check, interactive smoke, feature scenarios — for bash/zsh/fish/pwsh/nu. See `e2e/README.md`
for usage. Gotchas baked into it, relevant to any future pty work:

- PSReadLine on a raw Unix pty floods `CSI 6n` (DSR cursor-position) queries and wedges without a
  reply; ConPTY answers them internally on Windows. The harness's reader goroutine answers with
  the vt10x cursor position (`harness/session.go`).
- nu autoloads every `.nu` under `$nu.vendor-autoload-dirs` AFTER `--config`, so a dev machine's
  real oh-my-posh nu integration clobbers the test prompt. Isolate with `XDG_DATA_HOME` pointed
  at an empty dir (works on Windows too).
- go-pty's Windows `Cmd` resolves bare executable names relative to `Cmd.Dir` when `Dir` is set —
  always pass an absolute binary path.
- Windows PATH resolves `bash` to System32's WSL launcher, not Git Bash; it fails on Windows-style
  paths. `harness.LookupShellBinary` derives Git Bash from `git.exe`'s location.
- bash transient and rprompt are ble.sh-only (`bashBLEsession`, gated on `BLE_SESSION_ID` in
  `src/shell/bash.go`) — plain interactive bash gets no code for either feature.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Testing shells end-to-end

Patterns for functionally driving omp's shell integrations, mostly in WSL (verified on aarch64,
zsh 5.9, fish 4.1.2).

## WSL basics

- WSL `/tmp` is wiped between separate `wsl.exe` invocations (instance auto-shutdown). Either make
  a test fully self-contained in ONE `wsl -e` call, or stage everything under `$HOME` (e.g.
  `~/omp-test`).
- From Git Bash, prefix `MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1` so `/mnt/c/...` arguments reach wsl.exe unmangled.
- Build for WSL either inside WSL (`/usr/local/go/bin/go`) or cross-compile from Windows
  (`GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64`). Shell scripts are embedded at build time - rebuild after every
  script edit, then regenerate the sourced init with `oh-my-posh init <shell> --print`.
- Differential testing: build the control binary from `git show HEAD:<file>` or via
  `git stash push -- <file>`; the fixed binary from the working tree.
- `pkill -f <pattern>` matches its own caller's command line when inlined - pick patterns that
  cannot appear in the runner script.

## Getting a pty

- `zsh -i` under plain `wsl -e` hangs before the first prompt (no foreground pty). Options:
  - `wsl -e script -qec 'zsh -i' /dev/null` - real pty, MONITOR enabled, job-control behavior
    testable. Run backgrounded with output to a file and poll a done-marker; foreground runs can
    wedge under the wsl relay.
  - `zmodload zsh/zpty` in a driver zsh - best for keystroke-level scenarios: `zpty omp zsh -i`,
    write raw keys with `zpty -w -n omp $'\x03'` (Ctrl+C delivers a real SIGINT through the pty),
    assert via state files written by in-session commands, drain output with `zpty -r` after exit.
    Note `script(1)` does NOT work inside zpty (typescript never appears).
- Per-shell `script(1)` quirks: bash prompt bytes are not relayed to stdout (probe `${PS1@P}`
  in-session); fish discards piped typeahead (in-session probes never run); zsh relays fine.
- Do not test the zsh serve path from a non-interactive `zsh script.zsh` - it hangs in
  `read -u fd -d $'\0' -t N` (the `-t` is ignored with `-d`, see [zsh](zsh.md)).

## Driving vi-mode / keystroke scenarios (zpty pattern, verified 2026-07-14)

- Harness pattern (built for #5992): stage a binary, config, zdot dirs, and zpty driver scripts
  under `$HOME` in WSL; one driver runs mode-aware ESC/Enter/Ctrl+C scenarios into a state log,
  another asserts the transient prompt renders. Always compare a fixed run against a control zdot
  (same setup, feature under test disabled).
- Keep probes mode-aware: with zsh-vi-mode, a line after a normal-mode accept starts in normal
  mode - prefix typed commands with `i` where needed, and remember a stray self-inserted `i`
  turns the probe into a failing command (useful as a broken-state detector: `last_status=127`).

## Keeping a render alive

- There is no shell-command segment type. To hold `omp stream`/a render open for a controllable
  window, point an `http` segment at a silent local TCP listener
  (`python3 -c 'socket...accept...sleep'`) with a large `http_timeout`; kill the listener to
  trigger the async update record.

## Reading protocol streams

- One `bufio.Scanner` per pipe, ever - a second scanner on the same pipe loses buffered data
  (use a shared reader helper in Go tests).

content.content:
1	# Phase 6 — Deliver
2	
3	The change is verified; now package it.
4	
5	## Commits
6	
7	- Use the conventional-commit skill for every commit message.
8	- One logical unit per commit. A feature and its lint fallout can be separate commits when they
9	  answer different "why"s.
10	- Stage files explicitly — never `git add -A`.
11	- Review the staged diff before committing, especially after auto-fixing tools rewrote files.
12	
13	## Push and PR policy
14	
15	Do not push, force-push, or open a PR unless the user asked for it. "Commit" means commit;
16	nothing more. When a push is asked for on a rewritten branch, use `--force-with-lease`.
17	
18	## The final report

file.content:
# Phase 6 — Deliver

The change is verified; now package it.

## Commits

- Use the conventional-commit skill for every commit message.
- One logical unit per commit. A feature and its lint fallout can be separate commits when they
  answer different "why"s.
- Stage files explicitly — never `git add -A`.
- Review the staged diff before committing, especially after auto-fixing tools rewrote files.

## Push and PR policy

Do not push, force-push, or open a PR unless the user asked for it. "Commit" means commit;
nothing more. When a push is asked for on a rewritten branch, use `--force-with-lease`.

## The final report

Outcome first, then evidence. It contains:

1. What changed, with clickable file references, and why — one paragraph before any detail.
2. Where implementer output was overridden, and the reason.
3. Verification evidence: the gates that ran and the concrete functional results observed.
4. Loose ends explicitly left to the user (secrets to delete, manual validation steps, decisions
   deferred), each with the exact command or check when applicable.

Never report an unverified step as done, and never bury a failure in the middle of a success
story.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

content.text:
Done — the end-to-end shell test suite is built, verified on both platforms, and committed as three commits on `next`.

## What shipped

**[e2e/](e2e/README.md)** — a separate Go module (deps never touch `src/go.mod`) with three layers over the big five shells (bash, zsh, fish, pwsh, nu):

1. **Syntax** ([syntax_test.go](e2e/syntax_test.go)) — generates the init script per shell × feature overlay (base, transient, rprompt, tooltips, full) and validates it with the shell's own parser (`bash -n`, `zsh -n`, `fish --no-execute`, the pwsh language parser, `nu-check`). Placeholder-leak assertions run even when a shell isn't installed.
2. **Smoke** ([smoke_test.go](e2e/smoke_test.go)) — boots each shell interactively in a real pty (ConPTY on Windows, creack/pty elsewhere) via [session.go](e2e/harness/session.go), a go-pty + vt10x harness with rendered-screen and raw-byte assertions; asserts the prompt renders, a command runs, and `exit` actually terminates (which regression-covers the pwsh daemon exit deadlock).
3. **Behavior** ([features_test.go](e2e/features_test.go)) — exit-code propagation (`E2E:42>` after a failing command), transient prompt replacing the accepted line, rprompt right-aligned at column 120.

**[e2e.yml](.github/workflows/e2e.yml)** — PR workflow: ubuntu-latest runs all five shells (zsh/fish via apt, nu pinned to 0.113.1 — the version the harness was verified against), windows-latest runs pwsh + nu. **This workflow has not executed in CI yet** — it validates as YAML but its first real run will be on the next PR touching `src/**` or `e2e/**`; watch that run.

## Verification evidence

- Windows (this machine): full suite green — pwsh and nu pass all three layers for real, POSIX shells skip cleanly.
- WSL (linux/arm64, zsh 5.9, fish 4.1.2): full suite green — bash, zsh, fish, pwsh all pass; nu skips (not installed there).
- `gofmt`, `go vet`, `go mod tidy`, `golangci-lint` (0 issues); `src/` untouched.

## Things the implementers hit that you should know

- **PSReadLine on Unix ptys** floods `CSI 6n` cursor queries and wedges without replies (47s timeout → 2s pass once the harness answers them from the vt10x cursor). ConPTY masks this on Windows.
- **nu vendor autoload** runs *after* `--config`, so your real oh-my-posh nu integration clobbered the test prompt on this machine — isolated via `XDG_DATA_HOME`.
- **bash transient *and* rprompt are ble.sh-only** (`BLE_SESSION_ID` gate in [bash.go:41](src/shell/bash.go:41)) — both explicitly skipped for plain bash with comments.
- Windows PATH resolves `bash` to the System32 WSL launcher, not Git Bash — `LookupShellBinary` works around it.

All of these are recorded in the project-knowledge [testing.md](.agents/skills/project-knowledge/references/testing.md).

Natural next steps when you want them: cmd/clink coverage on the Windows job, tooltips/vi-mode/streaming scenarios on the existing harness, and the Docker version-matrix nightly.

content.thinking:
The user is asking about test execution time on Windows, noting that Docker layers aren't relevant since GitHub runners handle PRs fine. The full test suite runs in about 11-13 seconds, though there's some uncertainty around whether that timing includes the initial build of the omp binary, which happens during the test run itself. The `go test` command rebuilds the binary each time it runs, and the first build takes longer than subsequent cached builds.

I'm now looking at the actual measured times: Windows runs between 11.2–13.3 seconds total, while WSL takes 18.6–22 seconds. Breaking down the Windows run by test layer shows TestExitCode at 2.88s, TestTransient at 1.48s, TestRPrompt at 1.17s, TestSmoke at 1.34s, and TestSyntax at 4.13s. For CI environments, the picture changes significantly—cold Go module cache, building the omp binary from scratch, and installing dependencies via apt-get will dominate the runtime. I'm estimating setup takes 30-60 seconds for apt-get, another 2-3 minutes for Go setup and a cold build, with the tests themselves finishing in under a minute, putting the total job time around 4-5 minutes, which is comparable to the existing code.yml job. Rather than measuring a fresh cold build locally (which would require clearing the cache), I'll report the warm numbers I've measured plus give a reasonable estimate for CI performance.

content.text:
Locally the whole suite is fast — the shells themselves add very little:

- **Windows (this machine):** 11–13 seconds total for everything, including building the omp binary from `../src` (warm Go build cache). Breakdown from the `-v` run: syntax 4.1s, smoke 1.3s, exit-code 2.9s, transient 1.5s, rprompt 1.2s. Each interactive session costs roughly 0.5–2.5s (pwsh is the slowest to boot).
- **WSL/Linux:** 19–22 seconds total, with bash, zsh, fish, and pwsh all running interactively.

Individual sessions are bounded by the 30s prompt-wait and 15s exit deadlines, so a genuinely broken shell integration fails in under a minute rather than hanging the job.

In CI the test time will be dwarfed by setup: checkout, Go toolchain + module download (cold cache on first run), the one-time omp binary build (a minute or two cold), `apt-get install zsh fish`, and the nu download. Realistic total per job is around 3–5 minutes, in the same ballpark as the existing `Validate Code` job — the tests themselves should stay under a minute on the runners.

And agreed on dropping the Docker layer — if shell-version coverage ever matters (nu is the one that breaks compatibility regularly), it fits the existing workflow as a matrix dimension that installs a second pinned nu version, no containers needed.

input.new_string:
## Harness internals

Things the harness does that are easy to break by accident:

- **DSR replies** — PSReadLine on a Unix pty repeatedly queries the cursor position (`CSI 6n`)
  and blocks rendering until it gets a reply. ConPTY answers this internally on Windows; on
  Linux/macOS the harness itself replies with the vt10x cursor position from its reader
  goroutine (`harness/session.go`). Remove that and pwsh-on-Linux wedges until the 30s timeout.
- **Single pty reader** — exactly one goroutine reads the pty and feeds both the vt10x screen
  and the raw buffer under one mutex. A second reader silently loses buffered data.
- **nu vendor autoload** — nu loads every `.nu` under `$nu.vendor-autoload-dirs` after
  `--config`, so a machine with the real oh-my-posh nu integration installed would clobber the
  test prompt. Sessions point `XDG_DATA_HOME` at an empty temp directory to prevent this.
- **Absolute binary paths** — go-pty's Windows `Cmd` resolves bare executable names relative to
  `Cmd.Dir` when `Dir` is set, so `Start` always resolves the shell binary to an absolute path
  first.
- **bash lookup on Windows** — plain `PATH` lookup finds System32's WSL launcher `bash.exe`,
  which cannot run Windows-style script paths; `LookupShellBinary` derives Git Bash's location
  from `git.exe` instead.

## Known limitations

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# E2E Test Suite

End-to-end tests for the oh-my-posh shell integrations themselves (not the Go internals under
`src/`). They generate real init scripts with the `oh-my-posh` binary, feed them to the actual
shells, and drive interactive sessions in a pseudo-terminal.

This is a separate Go module (`github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/e2e`, own `go.mod`/`go.sum`)
so its dependencies never leak into `src/`.

## Layers

1. **Syntax** (`syntax_test.go`) — generate the init script per shell x config overlay and
   validate it with the shell's own parser (`bash -n`, `zsh -n`, `fish --no-execute`, a
   `System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser` call for pwsh, `nu-check` for nu).
2. **Smoke** (`smoke_test.go`) — boot each shell interactively in a real pty with the generated
   init script, assert the prompt renders cleanly, a typed command runs, and the shell exits.
3. **Behavior** (`features_test.go`) — per-feature scenarios: exit-code propagation, transient
   prompt, right prompt.

Both layers 2 and 3 drive the shell through `harness.Session`, a pty wrapper with a vt10x screen
emulator for rendered-screen assertions and a raw-byte buffer for escape-sequence assertions.

The suite targets the "big five" shells: bash, zsh, fish, pwsh, nu.

## Running locally

```shell
cd e2e
go test -count=1 ./...
```

Add `-v` for per-shell/per-overlay output, or `-run <Test>/<shell>` to scope to one case.

Every test skips cleanly (`t.Skip`) when a shell's binary is not on `PATH`, or when the shell is
not supported on the current platform:

| Shell | Linux/macOS                  | Windows                                                    |
|-------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| bash  | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped (msys/WSL bash under ConPTY is not representative) |
| zsh   | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped                                                    |
| fish  | yes (skip if binary missing) | skipped                                                    |
| pwsh  | yes (skip if binary missing) | yes (skip if binary missing)                               |
| nu    | yes (skip if binary missing) | yes (skip if binary missing)                               |

Layer 1 (syntax) still runs its non-empty/no-placeholder assertions unconditionally for every
shell; only the parser check itself is skipped when the binary is missing.

### Building the omp binary

The suite builds `../src` once per test run (guarded by `sync.Once`) into a temp directory and
uses that binary for every `init` invocation. Set `OMP_E2E_BINARY` to an absolute path to skip
the build and reuse a prebuilt binary instead — useful when iterating on tests without rebuilding
oh-my-posh every run.

### Isolation

Every omp invocation and shell session gets `OMP_CACHE_DIR` pointed at a fresh `t.TempDir()`, so
runs never share or pollute a developer's real cache. Sessions also fix `TERM=xterm-256color`
and a 120x30 pty size.

## Adding a new shell

Add an entry to the `Shells` table in `harness/shells.go`. A `ShellDef` needs:

- `Name` — the omp shell name passed to `oh-my-posh init <Name>`.
- `Binary` — the executable looked up via `LookupShellBinary`; missing means skip.
- `SyntaxCheck(scriptPath) *exec.Cmd` — a command that parses (not executes) the script and
  exits non-zero on a syntax error.
- `Launch(t, scriptPath, workDir) (bin string, args []string, env []string)` — how to boot the
  shell interactively with `scriptPath` sourced (e.g. a temp rc file plus `-i`).
- `Fail` — an `ExitCommand{Command, Code}`: a command line that reliably exits non-zero when
  typed interactively, and the exit code oh-my-posh should report in the next prompt.

If the shell can't be driven faithfully on every platform, add a case to
`ShellDef.SupportedOnHost` (see the bash/zsh/fish Windows exclusion for the reasoning).

Layers 1-3 all iterate `harness.Shells`, so a correctly filled-in entry is picked up everywhere
automatically — no test file needs editing for a new shell on its own.

## Adding a new feature scenario

1. Add an `Overlay` function to `harness/config.go` that mutates the base config map (see
   `Transient`, `RPrompt`, `Tooltips` for examples), and register it in `overlaySets` in
   `syntax_test.go` so layer 1 covers the new overlay's generated script.
2. Add a test (or a case in an existing `Test*` function) in `features_test.go`:
   - build the config with `harness.WriteConfig(t, harness.YourOverlay)`
   - `harness.Start(t, sh, cfgPath)` and `WaitForPrompt()`
   - drive the session (`SendLine`, `WaitFor`) and assert against `Screen()`/`ScreenLines()`
3. Skip shells that don't support the feature explicitly, with a comment explaining why (see the
   bash skips in `TestTransient`/`TestRPrompt` for the pattern), rather than silently succeeding
   or failing.

## Known limitations

- bash only renders a transient prompt or right prompt inside a `ble.sh` session (gated on
  `BLE_SESSION_ID`, see `src/shell/bash.go`); this harness's plain
  `bash --noprofile --rcfile ... -i` session doesn't provide one, so `TestTransient` and
  `TestRPrompt` skip bash explicitly.
- cmd, elvish, xonsh and yash are not covered by any layer.

## CI

`.github/workflows/e2e.yml` runs this suite on `ubuntu-latest` (bash, pwsh preinstalled; zsh and
fish installed via `apt`; nu installed from a pinned GitHub release) and on `windows-latest`
(pwsh preinstalled; nu installed from a pinned release; bash/zsh/fish skip by design).

lines: bash skips in `TestTransient`/`TestRPrompt` for the pattern), rather than silently succeeding
+Things the harness does that are easy to break by accident:
+- **DSR replies** — PSReadLine on a Unix pty repeatedly queries the cursor position (`CSI 6n`)
+  and blocks rendering until it gets a reply. ConPTY answers this internally on Windows; on
+  Linux/macOS the harness itself replies with the vt10x cursor position from its reader
+  goroutine (`harness/session.go`). Remove that and pwsh-on-Linux wedges until the 30s timeout.
+- **Single pty reader** — exactly one goroutine reads the pty and feeds both the vt10x screen
+  and the raw buffer under one mutex. A second reader silently loses buffered data.
+- **nu vendor autoload** — nu loads every `.nu` under `$nu.vendor-autoload-dirs` after
+  `--config`, so a machine with the real oh-my-posh nu integration installed would clobber the
+  test prompt. Sessions point `XDG_DATA_HOME` at an empty temp directory to prevent this.
+- **Absolute binary paths** — go-pty's Windows `Cmd` resolves bare executable names relative to
+  `Cmd.Dir` when `Dir` is set, so `Start` always resolves the shell binary to an absolute path
+- **bash lookup on Windows** — plain `PATH` lookup finds System32's WSL launcher `bash.exe`,
+  which cannot run Windows-style script paths; `LookupShellBinary` derives Git Bash's location
- bash only renders a transient prompt or right prompt inside a `ble.sh` session (gated on

content.thinking:
The real test here is whether the assertions validate what a user actually sees on their terminal—not just whether a function returns a string, but whether the shell properly executed the initialization script, invoked the binary at the prompt, and rendered the expected text like `E2E:0>` after interpreting all escape sequences. The exit code test confirms this further by checking that the prompt changes to `E2E:42>` when the shell exits with that code.

There are honest gaps though: the tests don't catch color or ANSI styling regressions since the config uses plain text, and features like FTCS marks, window titles, and cursor positioning aren't asserted on even though the raw buffer is captured. Segment correctness like git status contents is deliberately left to the Go unit tests—the e2e suite focuses on validating the shell integration itself with deterministic inputs.

content.text:
Done — [e2e/README.md](e2e/README.md) now carries the full detail including harness internals (the global README never had any e2e content; everything always lived in the e2e dir).

**Does it really test the output?** Yes, at the level that matters for the shell integration — with two honest gaps.

What the assertions actually run against is the *rendered screen* of a terminal emulator fed by the real pty byte stream. So a passing test proves the whole pipeline: the shell sourced the generated init script, its prompt hook invoked the real omp binary, the binary rendered the config, and the resulting bytes — after the terminal interpreted every escape sequence — painted the expected text in the expected place. Concretely:

- **Exit code** proves data flows through the integration: the prompt text changes from `E2E:0>` to `E2E:42>` only if the shell's prompt function passed `--status` correctly to the binary.
- **Transient** proves in-place repainting: the assertion is that the already-painted primary prompt line got *rewritten* on screen to `TR>` after Enter — that only passes if the cursor-movement and redraw sequences were emitted and interpreted correctly.
- **RPrompt** proves geometry: `RMARK` must end within 2 columns of column 120 on the same rendered row as the prompt.
- **Smoke** additionally asserts no error text appeared anywhere on screen.

The two gaps:

1. **Styling isn't asserted.** The test config uses `style: plain` text segments and we compare text only. vt10x does track color/attribute state per cell, so asserting foreground/background of the prompt cells is possible if you want color regressions caught — it just isn't done yet.
2. **Raw escape sequences aren't asserted.** `Session.Raw()` captures the unrendered byte stream precisely so scenarios like FTCS marks (`OSC 133`), PromptMark, or window-title sequences can be asserted directly — but today it's only used in failure dumps. Those are the natural next scenarios to add on the existing harness, alongside tooltips and vi-mode.

Segment *content* correctness (what a git segment would show, etc.) is deliberately not tested here — that stays with the Go unit tests; this suite pins the shell-side behavior with deterministic segments.

content.thinking:
The user wants me to add two test gaps: styling assertions for vt10x attribute colors in prompt cells, and raw escape-sequence assertions for FTCS marks and similar sequences. I should also refactor existing e2e tests into table-driven style where possible. The user has already approved moving forward with these additions, so I'm analyzing the requirements and planning the test implementation.

For the styling test, I need to verify how vt10x handles 24-bit truecolor codes — whether it stores RGB values directly in the Color type or if I should assert on the raw ANSI escape sequences instead. The latter approach (checking for SGR sequences like `[38;2;255;0;0m` in the output stream) is more robust and directly tests what omp emits, avoiding potential gaps in vt10x's truecolor support. Bash handles the escape markers transparently during readline display, so the actual color bytes reach the screen. For the test, I'll create a `TestPromptColor` overlay that adds a colored text segment with template "CLR" in red foreground and blue background, then assert the screen cells where "CLR" appears have the correct foreground color and the raw output contains the truecolor escape sequence. I need a Session helper method to retrieve the style at a specific marker location without exposing vt10x types to the test layer.

For the FTCS marks test, I need to figure out how to enable them — looks like there's a `shell_integration` config option at the root level that sets the FTCSMarks feature flag. I'll overlay that config and verify the marks appear in the output.

The test should boot the shell, wait for the prompt, then check that the raw output contains the FTCS escape sequences: `\x1b]133;A` for prompt start, `]133;B` for command start, `]133;C` for pre-exec, and `]133;D` for command finished. Since FTCS support varies by shell, I need to check which shells have implementations and skip those that don't.

For the assertions themselves, I should keep them modest: verify `]133;A` appears after the prompt, then after running a command and getting the next prompt, check for `]133;D` with the exit code and a second `]133;A`. The actual mark sequences are generated by the terminal package through methods like `PromptStart()` and `CommandStart()`.

For the test structure, I could refactor the feature tests into a table-driven approach where each row represents a scenario with its name, overlays, skip conditions, and run logic. This would create a scenario-by-shell matrix in one place, making it easier to test different shells against different scenarios. I'm leaning toward the table-driven approach with a scenario struct since it gives clearer -run filtering and better satisfies the requirement. The plan is to refactor features_test.go into a scenario table, add the color and ftcs scenarios with their skip conditions, create Session helpers for cell styling and assertions, and register the new Colored and ShellIntegration overlays in the harness config.

For the syntax test matrix, I need to include ShellIntegration since it changes the generated script, but skip Color since it only affects rendering. Before delegating, I should quickly verify the config knob for FTCS marks, which shells implement the feature, and check the vendored vt10x source for truecolor support handling.

content.text: Continuing the same workflow — quick analysis to pin the facts the implementer needs (how FTCS marks are enabled, which shells implement them, and whether vt10x tracks truecolor), then delegate.

input.description: Read vt10x SGR 38/48 truecolor handling

content.thinking:
I'm checking whether vt10x's Terminal interface provides per-cell access through a `Cell(x, y)` method, which would let me retrieve individual glyphs and their color data—specifically the truecolor values encoded as SGR 38;2;r;g;b sequences that map to RGB hex values like #ff0000.

For FTCS shell integration, I'm seeing that the config enables marks for bash, zsh, fish, and PowerShell, but not nushell. The marks being emitted are PromptStart (133;A), CommandStart/PromptEnd (133;B), and CommandFinished (133;D with optional exit code), but I haven't found evidence of 133;C (command output start) in the codebase yet—it might be emitted by shell preexec hooks or a separate script rather than omp itself.

input.command: grep -n "Cell\|func.*Glyph" "$(go env GOMODCACHE)/github.com/hinshun/vt10x@v0.0.0-20220301184237-5011da428d02/vt.go" | head; grep -rn "133;C\|133" /d/oh-my-posh/src/shell/scripts/*.{zsh,bash,fish,ps1} 2>/dev/null | head

content.thinking:
I've got the key implementation details locked in: the cell structure uses glyph data with foreground and background colors, where truecolor values are encoded as bit-shifted RGB. For FTCS support, I need shell integration enabled with bash, zsh, fish, and PowerShell all emitting the proper escape sequences—nu shell won't be supported. I'm planning to test across all five shells with color scenarios, so I should refactor the features test into a scenario table that covers both color and FTCS cases.

Now I'm working through the specifics: I'll add a second text segment to the prompt block with red foreground and blue background, then assert that the colored cells appear at the right position with the correct color values. I'm also adding a session helper to check cell colors, moving per-shell skip reasons into the scenario table as a map, and updating the README to document these new scenarios and remove outdated claims about styling assertions.

I'm thinking through potential complications: shells may reset colors between prompts, but the cells retain their attributes from when they were written. I also need to be careful about how omp renders the "plain" style—it might emit truecolor regardless of terminal capabilities, so if a terminal only supports 256-color mode, the assertion could fail and the implementer would need to adjust expectations at runtime.

For the FTCS scenario, I'm setting up the shell integration test: enable the feature, skip nu since it's not implemented, then verify the flow by checking for the FTCS markers in the raw output. I'll send an echo command and wait for the next prompt using a regex pattern to distinguish the output, then assert that the command and device status markers appear correctly.

I need to handle the CommandFinished code emission carefully — the D marker might include a code or be empty when the exit code is zero, so I'll just assert for the substring `]133;D` to match both cases. Bash emits the C marker through PROMPT0/DEBUG, and PowerShell does it via a PSReadLine key handler, which our PTY already supports based on the transient test.

Now I'm consolidating the test cases — merging TestExitCode, TestTransient, and TestRPrompt into a unified table structure. Smoke tests stay separate since they're the baseline with different assertions. TestRPrompt will call WaitForPrompt in the runner, then pass the session to the run function so it can access the screen directly. For naming, I can use the pattern `-run 'TestFeatures/rprompt/zsh'`. I need to register the shell_integration overlay in the syntax matrix since the script changes per shell, though the color overlay doesn't require script changes. For CI and docs, no changes are needed beyond updating the README to mention the color and FTCS scenarios in the layer-3 bullet.

Now I'm planning the verification approach: the implementer will handle Windows with PowerShell and Nushell, then I'll verify WSL, rerun the full suite, and lint everything. After that I can delegate the work and mark out the remaining tasks.

I've confirmed that `shell_integration: true` enables FTCS for bash/zsh/fish/pwsh, the scripts emit `133;A/B/C/D`, and vt10x tracks truecolor per cell with a `Cell(x, y)` API. Now I'll implement the delegation.

You are extending the e2e shell test suite in the oh-my-posh repository at D:\oh-my-posh (main working tree, do NOT branch or commit). The `e2e/` module is complete and green: read ALL of e2e/ first (harness/ + the three test files + README.md), plus the pinned spec at C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\f776f2bf-23df-4ccd-808c-738ef763c1c6\scratchpad\e2e-spec.md for background. Follow D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\golang\SKILL.md and, for the README edit, D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\markdown\SKILL.md.

Three goals:

## 1. Refactor features_test.go into ONE scenario table

Replace the separate TestExitCode/TestTransient/TestRPrompt functions with a single `TestFeatures` driven by a scenario table crossed with `harness.Shells` (subtests `TestFeatures/<scenario>/<shell>`). Shape (adapt as needed, keep it clean):

```go
type scenario struct {
    name     string
    overlays []harness.Overlay
    skips    map[string]string // shell name -> skip reason (declarative, replaces inline if-skips)
    run      func(t *testing.T, sh harness.ShellDef, s *harness.Session)
}
```

The runner does the common steps once per subtest: skip when a skip reason exists, WriteConfig with the scenario's overlays, harness.Start, WaitForPrompt, then hand off to run. Port the three existing scenarios' assertions unchanged (exit-code, transient, rprompt) with their existing skip reasons (bash: ble.sh-only for transient and rprompt). Do NOT restructure smoke_test.go or syntax_test.go beyond what goal 2 requires — they are already table-driven.

## 2. New scenario: prompt styling (screen-level color)

- Add a `Colored` overlay to harness/config.go: append a second text segment to the existing prompt block with `"foreground": "#ff0000"`, `"background": "#0000ff"`, `"template": "CLR"`, style plain.
- Add a Session helper in harness/session.go (same style as existing helpers) that locates the first occurrence of a marker string on the rendered screen and returns the vt10x foreground/background of its first cell — vt10x's Terminal has `Cell(x, y) Glyph` where x=column, y=row; take the session mutex while reading. Truecolor cells store `vt10x.Color(r<<16|g<<8|b)`, so `#ff0000` ⇒ 0xff0000 and `#0000ff` ⇒ 0x0000ff (verified in vt10x's state.go SGR 38;2/48;2 handling).
- Scenario `color` (all five shells): after WaitForPrompt, wait until `CLR` is on screen, then assert its cell's FG == 0xff0000 and BG == 0x0000ff.
- If the colors arrive as 256-palette values instead of truecolor, do NOT weaken the assertion — try setting `COLORTERM=truecolor` in the session env (harness Start) and re-verify; if that still doesn't give truecolor, STOP and report what the raw SGR sequence actually was.

## 3. New scenario: FTCS marks (raw escape-sequence level)

- Add a `ShellIntegration` overlay: sets `"shell_integration": true` at the config root (see src/config/config.go line ~81).
- Scenario `ftcs`, skipping nu with reason "shell_integration/FTCS marks are not implemented for nu (src/shell/nu.go)". Supported: bash, zsh, fish, pwsh — on Windows only pwsh actually runs; the orchestrator verifies the rest on Linux.
- Assertions against `s.Raw()`:
  - after the first prompt: contains `"\x1b]133;A"` (prompt start) and `"\x1b]133;B"` (prompt end / command start marker),
  - then SendLine("echo ftcs-check"), wait (regexp, (?s) style like the transient scenario) until the echo output AND a following `E2E:\d+>` prompt are both on screen,
  - then: Raw contains `"\x1b]133;C"` (pre-execution, emitted by the shell scripts — see src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1 ~line 1129, omp.zsh ~416, omp.bash ~105, omp.fish ~587) and `"\x1b]133;D"` (command finished; may or may not carry `;<code>` — assert the prefix only).
  - Verify for real on pwsh locally; if a mark genuinely never appears for a shell, STOP and report the raw tail instead of loosening the assertion.
- Register the ShellIntegration overlay in syntax_test.go's overlaySets (it changes the generated init script, so layer 1 must cover it). The Colored overlay does NOT need syntax coverage (it doesn't change the script), but adding it must not break anything if you have a reason to include it — default to leaving it out.

## Also

- Update e2e/README.md: the layers section (behavior layer now covers exit code, transient, rprompt, styling colors, FTCS marks), and the "Adding a new feature scenario" section to describe the scenario-table pattern instead of "add a test function". Keep the ble.sh limitation note; add the nu FTCS limitation to Known limitations. Run `npx markdownlint-cli2 e2e/README.md` and fix any findings.
- Never touch src/ or .github/.

Verification (must pass before reporting done):
- `cd D:\oh-my-posh\e2e && go build ./... && go vet ./... && gofmt -l .`
- `cd D:\oh-my-posh\e2e && go test -count=1 -v ./...` — on this Windows machine: pwsh must PASS color+ftcs for real, nu must PASS color and SKIP ftcs; all previously passing tests still pass; bash/zsh/fish platform-skip.
- Run the new scenarios 3 times (`-count=3 -run 'TestFeatures/(color|ftcs)'`) to shake out flakiness.

Report: files changed, per-shell per-scenario outcomes, the actual FG/BG values observed (hex) and whether COLORTERM was needed, which FTCS marks appeared for pwsh, and anything that didn't behave as specified (stop and report rather than improvise).

content.text:
All three goals are implemented and verified. Summary:

**Files changed** (all under `D:\oh-my-posh\e2e`, nothing under `src/` or `.github/` touched):
- `e2e/harness/config.go` — added `Colored` overlay (second text segment, `#ff0000`/`#0000ff`, template `CLR`, style plain) and `ShellIntegration` overlay (`cfg["shell_integration"] = true`).
- `e2e/harness/session.go` — added `Session.MarkerColor(marker string) (fg, bg vt10x.Color, found bool)`, which locks `s.mu`, scans `s.term.String()` line by line for the marker, and reads `s.term.Cell(x, y)` for its FG/BG.
- `e2e/features_test.go` — fully rewritten: removed `TestExitCode`/`TestTransient`/`TestRPrompt`, replaced with a `scenario` table (`name`, `overlays`, `skips map[string]string`, `run func(t, sh, s)`) and a single `TestFeatures` runner producing `TestFeatures/<scenario>/<shell>`. Five scenarios: `exit-code`, `transient`, `rprompt` (ported unchanged), plus new `color` and `ftcs`.
- `e2e/syntax_test.go` — registered `shell_integration` in `overlaySets` (not `Colored`, per spec, since it doesn't change the generated script).
- `e2e/README.md` — updated the layers list, rewrote "Adding a new feature scenario" for the table pattern, added the nu FTCS limitation next to the existing ble.sh note. `npx markdownlint-cli2 e2e/README.md` → 0 errors (max line length 101, within the 120 limit).

**Verification on this Windows machine:**
- `go build ./... && go vet ./... && gofmt -l .` → clean, no output.
- `go test -count=1 -v ./...` → all green. Per-shell/scenario outcomes: bash/zsh/fish skip (unsupported on Windows, per `SupportedOnHost`). pwsh and nu both PASS `exit-code`, `transient`, `rprompt`, `color`; pwsh PASSes `ftcs`; nu SKIPs `ftcs` with reason `"shell_integration/FTCS marks are not implemented for nu (src/shell/nu.go)"`. `TestSmoke` and `TestSyntax` (now including the new `shell_integration` overlay set for every shell) still pass/skip exactly as before.
- `go test -count=3 -run 'TestFeatures/(color|ftcs)'` → 3/3 clean, no flakiness.
- `cd src && go build ./...` → clean, confirming `src/` wasn't touched.

**Color scenario details:** FG/BG came back as true `0xff0000`/`0x0000ff` on the very first try — **no `COLORTERM` was needed**. I traced why: `src/terminal/writer.go` sets `color.TrueColor = Program != AppleTerminal` unconditionally in `Init()`, so truecolor SGR (`38;2;r;g;b`/`48;2;r;g;b`) is always emitted except when the detected terminal is Apple Terminal — it isn't gated by `COLORTERM` at all in this codebase. So I left the harness env alone rather than adding a `COLORTERM` var that would have been a no-op.

**FTCS marks for pwsh:** all four appeared as specified — `\x1b]133;A` and `\x1b]133;B` right after the first prompt (emitted by `src/prompt/primary.go` via `terminal.CommandFinished`/`PromptStart`, gated on `Config.ShellIntegration`, independent of shell), then after `echo ftcs-check` both `\x1b]133;C` (from the pwsh Enter key-handler, `src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1:1129`) and `\x1b]133;D` appeared in the raw buffer. I asserted `133;D` as a substring only (per instructions), since it may carry a `;<code>` suffix.

**Nothing deviated from spec** — no need to stop and report an anomaly; the only judgment call was confirming COLORTERM was genuinely unnecessary by reading the truecolor-detection code rather than assuming.
